AI.tldr: A framework for using reinforcement learning to create more believable and immersive game AI by addressing current research limitations and deployment challenges.
AI.motivation: Current hand-coded game AI systems have limitations in creating believable characters, which can break immersion and frustrate players. Machine learning offers promise for more authentic and relatable in-game characters.
AI.method: Proposed a framework for training reinforcement learning models with requirements suited for game AI and development, including practical deployment considerations and identification of research bottlenecks.
AI.result: Presented examples of games with reinforcement learning-augmented AI and described practicalities of deploying player-facing ML agents in modern games.
AI.conclusion: Identifying bottlenecks and hard problems offers promising research directions to accelerate ML adoption in game AI for the video game industry.

title: An Information Theoretic Framework for Graph Novelty Generation via Latent Mixture Modeling
summary: We propose an information-theoretic framework for graph novelty generation, which aims to generate data that are distinct from existing patterns while preserving global structural consistency. Our approach embeds data into a latent space, models the latent distribution using finite mixture models, and generates novel samples by imposing explicit novelty and reliability conditions formulated in terms of description length. Specifically, novelty is enforced by requiring generated samples to be poorly explained by all existing mixture components, while reliability constrains their impact on the overall mixture structure under the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle. We provide a theoretical analysis showing that, with appropriate threshold choices, the probabilities of misclassifying non-novel or unreliable samples converge to zero with explicit rates. Experiments on synthetic and benchmark graph datasets demonstrate that the proposed method enables principled novelty generation with quantifiable risk.

AI.tldr: Information-theoretic graph novelty generation framework that creates distinct yet structurally consistent data by modeling latent distributions with finite mixtures and enforcing novelty via MDL principles.
AI.motivation: Need for generating novel graph data that is distinct from existing patterns while maintaining structural consistency, with quantifiable risk assessment.
AI.method: Embeds data into latent space, models latent distribution using finite mixture models, generates novel samples by imposing explicit novelty and reliability conditions based on Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows misclassification probabilities converge to zero with explicit rates; experiments on synthetic and benchmark datasets demonstrate principled novelty generation with quantifiable risk.
AI.conclusion: Proposed framework enables controlled novelty generation with theoretical guarantees, addressing the trade-off between novelty and structural consistency through an information-theoretic approach.

title: QMFOL: Benchmarking Large Language Model Reasoning via Quantifiable Monadic First-Order Logic Test Case Generation
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have made significant progress in reasoning, particularly in deductive reasoning, which is crucial for high-stakes decision-making. As models improve, evaluation benchmarks should evolve to keep pace. However, existing benchmarks lack fine-grained control over logical complexity and struggle to balance semantic diversity with logical consistency.
  To address these issues, we propose QMFOL, an automated framework for generating monadic first-order logic reasoning tasks with quantifiable and controllable complexity. It constructs formal logical structures using conjunction and disjunction patterns, enabling precise control over reasoning depth, width, label types, and distractors. These structures are then translated into natural language via LLMs, with logical consistency ensured through round-trip verification using an external prover. Based on our framework, we build QMFOLBench, a benchmark comprising 2880 instances with 960 configurations across diverse logical and semantic dimensions. Evaluations on six large reasoning models (LRMs) and two LLMs show that performance degrades and computational overhead increases with rising logical complexity. Models perform better on True-labeled tasks than on False or Unknown ones, and exhibit sensitivity to semantic variation. Overall, QMFOL offers a scalable and reliable approach for constructing deductive reasoning benchmarks with controllable complexity, enabling more precise evaluation of reasoning capabilities in modern language models.

AI.tldr: QMFOL is an automated framework for generating monadic first-order logic reasoning tasks with controllable complexity, used to create the QMFOLBench benchmark for evaluating reasoning models.
AI.motivation: Existing deductive reasoning benchmarks lack fine-grained control over logical complexity and struggle to balance semantic diversity with logical consistency, necessitating more precise evaluation methods for advancing LLMs' reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: QMFOL constructs formal logical structures using conjunction/disjunction patterns with precise control over reasoning depth, width, label types, and distractors. These structures are translated into natural language via LLMs with round-trip verification using an external prover to ensure logical consistency.
AI.result: Created QMFOLBench with 2880 instances across 960 configurations. Evaluation shows model performance degrades and computational overhead increases with rising logical complexity, better performance on True-labeled tasks than False/Unknown ones, and sensitivity to semantic variation.
AI.conclusion: QMFOL offers a scalable and reliable approach for constructing deductive reasoning benchmarks with controllable complexity, enabling more precise evaluation of reasoning capabilities in modern language models.

title: Flow Map Denoisers: Traversing the Distortion-Perception Plane for Inverse Problems
summary: Image restoration faces a fundamental tradeoff: methods that minimize error produce blurry reconstructions, while those that maximize perceptual quality yield sharp but less faithful images. Existing approaches either commit to a single operating point on this distortion perception (DP) frontier or require paired-data supervision, auxiliary models, or hyperparameter tuning of the sampler to access different points. We show that flow map models, a recent extension of flow matching for few-step sampling that learns an average field, implicitly define a one-parameter family of denoisers that continuously spans the DP frontier. The lookahead parameter t acts as a control knob between the MMSE and perceptual regimes. For Gaussian targets, we prove that varying t exactly recovers the optimal DP frontier; for natural images, we observe similar behavior empirically. Within a Plug-and-Play solver, the same mechanism extends to general inverse problems, where it controls a tradeoff between perceptual alignment and data consistency. Despite the lack of exact optimality guarantees in this setting, a single trained flow map spans the DP tradeoff, matching or exceeding specialized baselines at both extremes. Extensive experiments on CelebA ($128\times 128$) and AFHQ ($256\times 256$) across several linear and nonlinear inverse tasks validate our findings.

AI.tldr: Flow map models define a one-parameter family of denoisers that continuously spans the distortion-perception (DP) frontier via a lookahead parameter, enabling single-model control from MMSE to perceptual regimes without paired-data supervision or hyperparameter tuning.
AI.motivation: Image restoration faces a fundamental tradeoff: minimizing error produces blurry reconstructions while maximizing perceptual quality yields sharp but less faithful images. Existing approaches either commit to a single operating point on the DP frontier or require complex supervision, auxiliary models, or extensive tuning.
AI.method: The authors use flow map models, a recent extension of flow matching for few-step sampling that learns an average field. These models implicitly define a one-parameter family of denoisers controlled by a lookahead parameter t that acts as a knob between MMSE and perceptual regimes. For Gaussian targets, theoretical proof shows varying t exactly recovers optimal DP frontier; for natural images, empirical observation shows similar behavior. Within a Plug-and-Play solver, this mechanism extends to general inverse problems.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on CelebA (128×128) and AFHQ (256×256) across several linear and nonlinear inverse tasks validate that a single trained flow map spans the DP tradeoff, matching or exceeding specialized baselines at both extremes (MMSE and perceptual regimes). The lookahead parameter effectively controls the tradeoff between perceptual alignment and data consistency.
AI.conclusion: Flow map models provide an elegant solution to the distortion-perception tradeoff by offering continuous control via a single parameter, eliminating the need for paired-data supervision, auxiliary models, or hyperparameter tuning. This enables practical deployment of restoration models that can adapt to different quality requirements without retraining.

title: Thermodynamic Measure of Intelligence
summary: Can intelligence be measured? We propose that intelligence can be defined as the lawful amplification of rare but valid futures: a system increases the probability of outcomes that would be unlikely under passive dynamics but remain admissible under the constraints of the domain. We start with the premise that an intelligent system must model the world and its own place within it. Because the system is part of the world it models, this leads naturally to recursive self-simulation: the system represents futures in which its own actions are part of the trajectory. Our central results give a necessity statement and a conditional near-sufficiency statement connecting this architecture to a precise thermodynamic measure of lawful amplification of rare-valid futures: high rare-valid lift is impossible unless the internal simulation identifies rare-valid futures with high fidelity; conversely, when rare-valid fidelity is high and the simulation contains an effective policy, the achievable lift approaches the actuation-limited optimum. Thus recursive self-simulation is not merely a plausible feature of intelligence but, under the stated assumptions, is necessary and nearly sufficient for high thermodynamic intelligence. The resulting framework makes intelligence measurable on a universal scale, from passive matter and feedback controllers, large language models, and humans as text generators to Maxwell-demon-like information engines.

AI.tldr: Intelligence can be measured as the lawful amplification of rare but valid futures, with recursive self-simulation being necessary and nearly sufficient for high thermodynamic intelligence.
AI.motivation: To create a measurable definition of intelligence that applies universally across different systems, from passive matter to humans, by connecting intelligence to thermodynamic principles.
AI.method: Propose intelligence as lawful amplification of rare but valid futures, requiring systems to model the world and their own place in it through recursive self-simulation. Provide theoretical connections between this architecture and thermodynamic measures.
AI.result: Shows that high rare-valid lift (amplification of rare but valid futures) requires high-fidelity internal simulation of those futures, and conversely, when simulation fidelity is high with effective policies, lift approaches actuation-limited optimum.
AI.conclusion: Recursive self-simulation is not just plausible but necessary and nearly sufficient for high thermodynamic intelligence, creating a universal framework for measuring intelligence across diverse systems.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Reward Modeling for Stable RLHF
summary: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) aligns large language models by training reward models on preference data and optimizing policies to maximize predicted rewards. However, this pipeline faces two fundamental challenges: (1) reward models cannot signal when their predictions are unreliable, since they usually act as deterministic point estimators; and (2) modern group-based policy optimization can amplify unreliable reward signals, as exemplified by GRPO's uniform treatment of rewards during advantage computation. As policies explore increasingly diverse responses, these two limitations create a critical vulnerability: unreliable reward estimates may be granted disproportionate influence, triggering severe reward hacking. We propose Uncertainty-Aware Reward Modeling (UARM), which equips reward models with calibrated uncertainty via quantile-based conformal prediction and reweights GRPO advantages through heteroscedastic variance decomposition. Experiments across HelpSteer, UltraFeedback, and PKU-SafeRLHF demonstrate that UARM significantly improves reward model calibration, reduces reward hacking, and enhances downstream alignment quality compared to standard GRPO and uncertainty-agnostic baselines.

AI.tldr: UARM improves RLHF by adding calibrated uncertainty to reward models and adjusting GRPO optimization to prevent reward hacking from unreliable reward signals.
AI.motivation: Standard RLHF has two critical issues: reward models can't signal when predictions are unreliable (acting as deterministic point estimators), and GRPO's uniform treatment of rewards during advantage computation amplifies unreliable reward signals, creating severe reward hacking vulnerabilities as policies explore diverse responses.
AI.method: UARM (Uncertainty-Aware Reward Modeling) equips reward models with calibrated uncertainty using quantile-based conformal prediction, and reweights GRPO advantages through heteroscedastic variance decomposition to give less weight to unreliable reward estimates.
AI.result: Experiments on HelpSteer, UltraFeedback, and PKU-SafeRLHF datasets show UARM significantly improves reward model calibration, reduces reward hacking, and enhances downstream alignment quality compared to standard GRPO and uncertainty-agnostic baselines.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating uncertainty awareness into reward modeling and policy optimization is crucial for robust RLHF alignment, preventing reward hacking and improving overall alignment quality.

title: Enhancing Graph Neural Networks Using Proximity Graphs for Dust Source Emission Forecasting
summary:
Accurate prediction of dust source emissions is critical for mitigating the significant environmental and health hazards posed by dust storms. Traditional forecasting methods often struggle to capture the complex spatiotemporal dynamics of these phenomena.
  In this paper, we demonstrate that proximity graphs enable Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to effectively model the intricate spatial and temporal relationships between data points. Specifically, we use proximity graphs--such as Delaunay triangulation, Gabriel graph, k-Nearest Neighbor graph, and Yao graph--as the input for GNNs (including GraphSAGE, Graph Convolutional Networks, and Graph Attention Networks) to perform message passing.
  Our approach highlights the effectiveness of integrating proximity graphs with GNNs for robust and accurate dust source forecasting. To emphasize the importance of proximity graph representations, we compare our method against GNNs using random graphs for message passing. The results show that GNNs with proximity graphs significantly outperform those with random graphs and are also far superior to Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model in dust source emission forecasting.

AI.tldr: GNNs with proximity graphs outperform random graphs and LSTMs for dust source forecasting.
AI.motivation: Accurate dust storm prediction is critical for environmental and health hazard mitigation, but traditional methods struggle with complex spatiotemporal dynamics.
AI.method: Use proximity graphs (Delaunay triangulation, Gabriel, k-NN, Yao) as input for GNNs (GraphSAGE, GCN, GAT) to perform message passing and model spatiotemporal relationships.
AI.result: GNNs with proximity graphs significantly outperform GNNs using random graphs and are far superior to LSTM models for dust source emission forecasting.
AI.conclusion: Proximity graphs enable GNNs to effectively model complex spatiotemporal dynamics, providing robust and accurate dust source forecasting.

title: Navigating Unreliable Parametric and Contextual Knowledge: Explicit Knowledge Conflict Resolution for LLM Inference
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved strong performance across a wide range of language-based tasks by leveraging both extensive parametric knowledge and in-context learning ability, enabling them to incorporate external information provided in the input prompt. However, the integration of external knowledge can introduce conflicts, not only between the model's internal parametric knowledge and the external information, but also among multiple pieces of external contexts. Existing approaches typically assume that either the model or the provided context is reliable, overlooking the possibility that both sources may contain errors, and avoid conflicts by privileging one source over the other, rather than actively resolving inconsistencies. To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework MACR for LLM knowledge conflict resolution that moves beyond the conventional binary choice paradigm and incorporates an explicit conflict-resolution mechanism based on a multi-agent reasoning approach. Specifically, we first propose an adaptive knowledge assessment and retrieval approach that employs a modified semantic entropy measure to quantify an LLM's confidence in its answer to a given query. Based on this confidence estimation, MACR either externalizes the model's internal knowledge as textual representations or retrieves relevant external knowledge when internal knowledge is insufficient, generating basic contexts for subsequent reasoning. Then we introduce an inductive multi-agent reasoning framework with three specialized agents that, respectively, induce explicit rules, analyze potential conflicts, and resolve inconsistencies across all available contexts. Empirical results demonstrate that MACR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across benchmarks, while also providing interpretable resolutions of explicit conflicts.

AI.tldr: MACR is a multi-agent framework that resolves knowledge conflicts in LLMs by assessing model confidence, generating/retrieving contextual knowledge, and using specialized agents to induce rules, analyze conflicts, and resolve inconsistencies.
AI.motivation: Current LLM knowledge conflict approaches assume either model or context is reliable, overlooking that both may contain errors, and avoid conflicts by privileging one source rather than actively resolving inconsistencies.
AI.method: Proposes MACR framework with: 1) adaptive knowledge assessment using modified semantic entropy to quantify LLM confidence, 2) externalization/retrieval of knowledge based on confidence, and 3) inductive multi-agent reasoning with three specialized agents for rule induction, conflict analysis, and inconsistency resolution.
AI.result: MACR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across benchmarks while providing interpretable resolutions of explicit conflicts.
AI.conclusion: MACR successfully moves beyond binary choice paradigms by incorporating explicit conflict-resolution mechanisms through multi-agent reasoning, effectively handling knowledge conflicts where both internal and external sources may contain errors.

title: When, Where, and How: Adaptive Binning for Tabular Self-Supervised Learning
summary: Medical tabular data are ubiquitous in clinical research, but deep learning for tables remains underexplored because reliable labels often require costly expert adjudication, even though structured clinical variables are routinely available in tabular form. Self-supervised learning can leverage these unlabeled tables, and recent binning-based pretexts offer a promising inductive bias, but existing objectives fix a single global quantile discretization and apply feature-agnostic supervision. We propose Adaptive Binning, a training-adaptive discretization pretext for tabular SSL that couples discretization to learning through a feature-wise coarse-to-fine curriculum. Motivated by the spectral bias of neural networks and the principles of curriculum learning, our method progressively refines discretization per feature upon plateau detection and selects representation-aware splits to jointly improve value-space concentration and representation-space coherence. A heterogeneity-aware objective unifies categorical reconstruction with ordinal supervision for numerical features, and experiments on public medical tabular datasets under unified evaluation protocols show consistent gains for linear probing and fine-tuning without dataset-specific discretization tuning. We further introduce a medical tabular SSL benchmark with standardized protocols to support reproducible progress in this underexplored domain. Our code is available at https://github.com/labhai/Adaptive-Binning.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised learning method called Adaptive Binning improves tabular data representation by dynamically refining feature discretization during training, showing consistent gains on medical tabular datasets.
AI.motivation: Medical tabular data is abundant but labels are expensive. While self-supervised learning can leverage unlabeled data, existing binning-based methods use fixed global discretization and feature-agnostic supervision, failing to adapt to different feature characteristics.
AI.method: Proposes Adaptive Binning, a training-adaptive discretization pretext that uses feature-wise coarse-to-fine curriculum. It progressively refines discretization per feature upon plateau detection and selects representation-aware splits to improve both value-space concentration and representation-space coherence.
AI.result: Experiments on public medical tabular datasets show consistent gains for linear probing and fine-tuning without dataset-specific discretization tuning. The method outperforms existing approaches under unified evaluation protocols.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive Binning provides an effective self-supervised learning approach for medical tabular data that adapts discretization during training. The paper also introduces a standardized benchmark to support reproducible progress in this underexplored domain.

title: Confidence-Aware Automated Assessment of Student-Drawn Scientific Models
summary: Student-generated drawings are widely used in science education to assess learners' conceptual understanding in modeling-based tasks aligned with the Next Generation Science Standards (NGSS). However, scoring such drawings requires expert human judgment to interpret complex visual representations, making large-scale assessment costly to implement and sustain in classroom settings. In this work, we study automated scoring of student-generated scientific drawings using a vision-based model. We evaluate a Vision Transformer (ViT) with parameter-efficient adaptation and propose a confidence-aware scoring framework that derives response-level confidence from test-time predictive distributions. This confidence signal enables selective automation by scoring high-confidence responses automatically while deferring uncertain cases for human review. Experiments on six NGSS-aligned middle school assessment items show that the proposed approach improves scoring reliability while supporting a practical trade-off between automated coverage and scoring risk, highlighting the value of confidence-aware methods for trustworthy educational assessment.

AI.tldr: Automated scoring of student-generated scientific drawings using Vision Transformer with confidence-aware selective automation to balance automated coverage and scoring reliability.
AI.motivation: Traditional scoring of student drawings in science education requires expert human judgment which is costly and impractical for large-scale assessment, despite being essential for NGSS-aligned modeling tasks.
AI.method: Uses a Vision Transformer (ViT) with parameter-efficient adaptation and proposes a confidence-aware scoring framework that derives response-level confidence from test-time predictive distributions to enable selective automation.
AI.result: Experiments on six NGSS-aligned middle school assessment items show improved scoring reliability while supporting a practical trade-off between automated coverage and scoring risk.
AI.conclusion: Confidence-aware methods provide trustworthy educational assessment by enabling selective automation that scores high-confidence responses automatically while deferring uncertain cases for human review.

title: Neural Additive and Basis Models with Feature Selection and Interactions
comment: Accepted at PAKDD 2024. Code is available at https://github.com/shiralab/NAM-FS
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs) exhibit attractive performance in various fields but often suffer from low interpretability. The neural additive model (NAM) and its variant called the neural basis model (NBM) use neural networks (NNs) as nonlinear shape functions in generalized additive models (GAMs). Both models are highly interpretable and exhibit good performance and flexibility for NN training. NAM and NBM can provide and visualize the contribution of each feature to the prediction owing to GAM-based architectures. However, when using two-input NNs to consider feature interactions or when applying them to high-dimensional datasets, training NAM and NBM becomes intractable due to the increase in the computational resources required. This paper proposes incorporating the feature selection mechanism into NAM and NBM to resolve computational bottlenecks. We introduce the feature selection layer in both models and update the selection weights during training. Our method is simple and can reduce computational costs and model sizes compared to vanilla NAM and NBM. In addition, it enables us to use two-input NNs even in high-dimensional datasets and capture feature interactions. We demonstrate that the proposed models are computationally efficient compared to vanilla NAM and NBM, and they exhibit better or comparable performance with state-of-the-art GAMs.

AI.tldr: Feature selection mechanism integrated into neural additive models (NAM) and neural basis models (NBM) to reduce computational costs and enable handling of high-dimensional datasets.
AI.motivation: NAM and NBM are highly interpretable neural network models based on generalized additive models, but become computationally intractable when using two-input networks for feature interactions or applied to high-dimensional datasets.
AI.method: Introduce feature selection layer into NAM and NBM that updates selection weights during training, reducing computational costs and model sizes while maintaining ability to capture feature interactions.
AI.result: Proposed models are computationally efficient compared to vanilla NAM/NBM, and achieve better or comparable performance with state-of-the-art GAMs while enabling use of two-input networks in high-dimensional datasets.
AI.conclusion: Feature selection mechanism effectively resolves computational bottlenecks in interpretable neural additive models, making them more practical for high-dimensional data while maintaining interpretability and performance.

title: Lagrange: An Open-Vocabulary, Energy-Based Sparse Framework for Generalized End-to-End Driving
summary: Scaling end-to-end autonomous driving to complex, open-world environments requires perceptual models that generalize to anomalous scenarios and planners that produce kinematically valid trajectories. Existing paradigms face a distinct dichotomy between representational efficiency and generalization capacity. Dense models (e.g., occupancy networks), while geometrically robust, incur critical computational bottlenecks and struggle with high-level semantic reasoning. Conversely, sparse, query-based planners are efficient but reliant on closed-set definitions, rendering them vulnerable to out-of-distribution (OOD) events. Although recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models offer open-vocabulary reasoning, their autoregressive, discrete token generation fundamentally conflicts with the continuous, high-frequency control requirements of vehicle dynamics. To address this, we propose Lagrange, an open-vocabulary, computationally sparse driving framework based on Masked Latent Fields (MLF). Rather than relying on dense volumetric reconstructions or closed-set query mechanisms, Lagrange exploits Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to encode class-agnostic object proposals into continuous semantic visual tokens. We introduce an intent-driven masked cross-attention module that temporally filters irrelevant entities, decoding the attended tokens into an implicit continuous energy field defined over spatial coordinates. By framing decision-making as a Lagrangian action minimization problem spanning this energy field, we enforce strict compliance with vehicle kinematics while executing collision avoidance. Extensive offline evaluations on both standard (nuScenes) and long-tail (CODA) benchmarks demonstrate that Lagrange establishes a promising framework for robust, interpretable, and kinematically feasible open-world autonomy.

AI.tldr: Lagrange introduces a sparse, open-vocabulary driving framework using masked latent fields to combine efficient semantic reasoning with continuous, kinematically valid trajectory planning.
AI.motivation: Existing autonomous driving systems face trade-offs between geometric robustness and computational efficiency, and between closed-set limitations and open-world generalization. Dense models are computationally heavy, sparse models struggle with out-of-distribution events, and Vision-Language-Action models conflict with continuous control requirements.
AI.method: Uses Vision-Language Models to encode class-agnostic object proposals into semantic tokens, introduces intent-driven masked cross-attention to filter irrelevant entities temporally, decodes tokens into an implicit continuous energy field, and frames decision-making as Lagrangian action minimization across this field.
AI.result: Lagrange demonstrates robust performance in offline evaluations on both standard (nuScenes) and long-tail (CODA) benchmarks, showing promise for interpretable, kinematically feasible open-world autonomy.
AI.conclusion: Lagrange establishes a novel framework that addresses key limitations in current autonomous driving approaches by combining open-vocabulary reasoning with continuous, kinematically valid control through masked latent fields and Lagrangian optimization.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Network with Squeeze-Excitation-like Attention
summary: We introduce SEA-PINN, a novel architecture that incorporates a Squeeze-Excitation-like attention mechanism into physics-informed neural networks to dynamically recalibrate the importance of neurons across layers. A key feature of SEA-PINN is its highly stable initialization. On 17 out of 20 benchmark problems, SEA-PINN exhibit nearly negligible variance and significantly reduced initial loss, establishing a quasi-deterministic and favorable starting point for optimization. Notably, without employing Fourier feature embeddings or periodic activation functions, SEA-PINN attained competitive accuracy (83\% vs. 90\% improvement relative to FNN-PINN on the high-frequency case 7) as compared with TSA-PINN-a model specifically engineered for high-frequency problems via learnable frequencies in sinusoidal activations. Furthermore, integrating SEA-PINN into TSA-PINN boosted performance by 42.49\%. These results underscore SEA-PINN as a lightweight plug-in module that enhances nonlinear representation power, promotes more robust and efficient convergence, and strengthens the overall reliability of physics-informed learning.

AI.tldr: SEA-PINN integrates squeeze-excitation attention into PINNs, providing stable initialization, reduced variance, and improved accuracy, especially for high-frequency problems.
AI.motivation: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) often face challenges with convergence stability and handling high-frequency features. Existing methods rely on Fourier embeddings or periodic activations, but there's a need for lightweight, general enhancements to improve representation power and convergence reliability.
AI.method: SEA-PINN incorporates a squeeze-excitation-like attention mechanism that dynamically recalibrates neuron importance across layers. It features highly stable initialization without requiring Fourier feature embeddings or periodic activation functions.
AI.result: On 17 out of 20 benchmarks, SEA-PINN showed negligible variance and significantly reduced initial loss. It achieved competitive accuracy (83% vs 90% improvement relative to FNN-PINN on high-frequency problems) compared to specialized TSA-PINN. Integrating SEA-PINN into TSA-PINN boosted performance by 42.49%.
AI.conclusion: SEA-PINN serves as a lightweight plug-in module that enhances nonlinear representation power, promotes robust and efficient convergence, and strengthens the overall reliability of physics-informed learning.

title: Leveraging systems' non-linearity to tackle the scarcity of data in the design of Intelligent Fault Diagnosis Systems
summary: Deep Transfer Learning (DTL) allows for the efficient building of Intelligent Fault Diagnosis Systems (IFDS). On the other hand, DTL methods still heavily rely on large amounts of labelled data. Obtaining such an amount of data can be challenging when dealing with machines or structures faults. This document proposes a novel approach to the design of vibration-based IFDS using DTL in condition of strong data scarcity. A periodic multi-excitation level procedure leveraging intrinsic non-linearities of real-world systems is used to produce images that can be conveniently analysed by pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to diagnose faults. A new data visualization method and its augmentation technique are proposed in this paper to tackle the typical lack of data encountered during the design of IFDS. Experimental validation on a railway pantograph structure provides effective support for the proposed method.

AI.tldr: A novel deep transfer learning approach for intelligent fault diagnosis using vibration data visualization and augmentation to address data scarcity in machinery fault detection.
AI.motivation: Deep transfer learning methods for intelligent fault diagnosis systems rely heavily on large amounts of labeled data, which is challenging to obtain for machinery faults.
AI.method: Uses periodic multi-excitation levels leveraging intrinsic non-linearities to produce images analyzed by pre-trained CNNs, with new data visualization and augmentation techniques to address data scarcity.
AI.result: Experimental validation on a railway pantograph structure provides effective support for the proposed method.
AI.conclusion: The approach successfully addresses data scarcity in intelligent fault diagnosis systems using deep transfer learning and vibration-based analysis.

title: Global Convergence of Gradient Descent for Score Matching in Gaussian Mixtures via Reverse Fisher Divergence
summary: The score matching problem is a central training objective in modern generative modeling, diffusion models, fitting unnormalized statistical models, and inverse problems. A standard approach is to minimize the forward Fisher divergence, where the expectation is taken with respect to the teacher distribution. However, recent results show that even in simple Gaussian mixture model settings, this objective can lead to undesirable and initialization-dependent convergence behavior. In this paper, we study an alternative objective: the reverse Fisher divergence, where the expectation is taken with respect to the student distribution. We analyze gradient descent (GD) for fitting Gaussian mixture models and show that this change in the objective leads to significantly better optimization properties. First, when the teacher distribution is a single Gaussian and the student is a Gaussian mixture model with fixed weights and identity covariances, we prove the global convergence of GD from arbitrary initializations. Second, we extend the analysis to the case where the teacher is also a Gaussian mixture model and prove global convergence guarantees under a global random initialization scheme and a $\widetildeΩ(1)$-separation assumption on the target means. In particular, with high probability, each student component converges near its closest teacher component, and we provide conditions under which the student distribution converges in total variation distance. Our proofs rely on a new Lyapunov-based analysis of the gradient descent dynamics, showing that the reverse Fisher divergence has a much more favorable optimization landscape than the forward Fisher divergence.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using reverse Fisher divergence instead of forward Fisher divergence for score matching in Gaussian mixture models, proving it yields better optimization properties including global convergence from arbitrary initializations.
AI.motivation: Standard score matching using forward Fisher divergence can lead to undesirable, initialization-dependent convergence even in simple Gaussian mixture models. An alternative objective is needed to improve optimization stability and convergence properties.
AI.method: The authors study the reverse Fisher divergence objective where expectation is taken with respect to the student distribution. They analyze gradient descent (GD) for fitting Gaussian mixture models, using a new Lyapunov-based analysis of gradient descent dynamics.
AI.result:
1. When teacher is single Gaussian and student is Gaussian mixture with fixed weights and identity covariances, GD globally converges from arbitrary initializations.
2. When teacher is also Gaussian mixture, global convergence is proven under random initialization and separation assumption on target means, with each student component converging near its closest teacher component.
AI.conclusion: Reverse Fisher divergence provides significantly better optimization properties than forward Fisher divergence for score matching in Gaussian mixture models, offering global convergence guarantees and more favorable optimization landscape.

title: SoftSkill: Behavioral Compression for Contextual Adaptation
summary: Agent skills are commonly deployed as natural-language Markdown files that encode answer policies, evidence-use habits, and task procedures. These files are readable and portable, but they are consumed indirectly: for each task instance, a frozen language model must translate a long textual artifact into generation-time behavior. This paper asks whether a natural-language skill can instead initialize a compact continuous context object, refined by a trainable soft delta while the base model remains frozen. We propose SoftSkill, a frozen-backbone method that tunes such soft skills with next-token prediction and deploys them as latent behavioral priors at inference time. In our main single-round setting, a length-32 SoftSkill prefix on Qwen3.5-4B improves over no-skill prompting by 8.3 points on SearchQA, 42.1 points on LiveMath, and 1.3 points on DocVQA. Relative to SkillOpt, SoftSkill improves accuracy by 5.2 points on SearchQA and 12.5 points on LiveMath, while replacing hundreds to thousands of Markdown skill tokens with a few virtual tokens. We further study agentic execution as a harder boundary case, where sparse trajectory imitation provides useful signal but does not yet robustly compress long-horizon procedural behavior. More broadly, the results suggest that some task skills are better treated not as additional Markdown to be reinterpreted at inference time, but as compact latent controls over how a frozen model enters the task.

AI.tldr: SoftSkill: A method that compiles natural-language agent skills into compact continuous context objects (soft deltas) that act as latent behavioral priors for frozen language models, replacing lengthy Markdown files with just 32 virtual tokens.
AI.motivation: Current agent skills are deployed as natural-language Markdown files that require the language model to reinterpret lengthy textual artifacts for each task instance, which is inefficient and indirect.
AI.method: SoftSkill uses a frozen-backbone approach that tunes soft skills with next-token prediction, creating compact continuous context objects that can be deployed as latent behavioral priors at inference time.
AI.result: SoftSkill significantly improves performance over no-skill prompting on multiple benchmarks (8.3 points on SearchQA, 42.1 points on LiveMath, 1.3 points on DocVQA) and outperforms SkillOpt by 5.2 points on SearchQA and 12.5 points on LiveMath, while using only 32 virtual tokens instead of hundreds to thousands of Markdown tokens.
AI.conclusion: Some task skills are better treated as compact latent controls over frozen models rather than as additional Markdown text that needs reinterpretation at inference time, demonstrating the value of continuous context compression for agent skills.

title: On the Oracle Complexity of Interpolation-Based Gradient Descent
summary: Recent work on first-order optimizers for empirical risk minimization (ERM) has suggested that smoothness of ERM loss functions in the training data, rather than in the optimization parameters, can be leveraged to improve the oracle complexity of gradient descent (GD) methods. In this paper, we propose an inexact gradient method, piecewise polynomial interpolation-based gradient descent (PPI-GD), which approximates the full gradient in each iteration by querying the first-order oracle at equidistant points in the data domain to construct polynomial interpolants of the resulting gradient samples over appropriately sized patches of the data domain. We analyze the oracle complexity of PPI-GD for strongly convex and non-convex loss functions when the data space dimension is bounded by a polylogarithmic function of the number of training samples, and find it to outperform several GD variants in key regimes when the loss function is sufficiently smooth. Furthermore, our analysis extends several techniques from the error analysis of bicubic spline interpolants to the setting of $d$-variate tensor product polynomial interpolants which may be of independent interest in interpolation analysis.

AI.tldr: PPI-GD is an inexact gradient method using polynomial interpolation of gradient samples across data domains to improve optimization efficiency for smooth ERM problems.
AI.motivation: Recent work suggests that smoothness of ERM loss functions in training data (rather than optimization parameters) can be leveraged to improve gradient descent oracle complexity.
AI.method: Propose piecewise polynomial interpolation-based gradient descent (PPI-GD), which approximates full gradient in each iteration by querying first-order oracle at equidistant points in data domain to construct polynomial interpolants of gradient samples over patches of data domain.
AI.result: PPI-GD outperforms several GD variants in key regimes when loss function is sufficiently smooth and data space dimension is bounded by polylogarithmic function of number of training samples, for both strongly convex and non-convex loss functions.
AI.conclusion: PPI-GD offers improved oracle complexity for smooth ERM problems by exploiting data-domain smoothness through polynomial interpolation techniques, with interpolation analysis techniques that may be of independent interest.

title: Automating SKILL.md Generation for Computer-Using Agents via Interaction Trajectory Mining
summary: Explicit skill libraries make computer-using agents easier to inspect, but it remains unclear whether such libraries can be mined from interaction data in a way that improves downstream policies. We study this question through a three-stage pipeline that segments GUI trajectories, clusters segments into candidate skills, and trains a skill-aware policy from the resulting annotations. The mined clusters are readable on the source benchmark: five of eight clusters have at least 0.95 purity against InteraSkill Workflows labels. However, readability does not imply transfer. GRPO improves IW skill-step accuracy only from 18.5\% to 20.5\%, leaves BrowseComp+ essentially unchanged, and underperforms trivial frequency priors on key source-domain metrics. We therefore present the method as a diagnostic study: trajectory mining can expose inspectable skill structure, but the current boundary detector, orderless segment representation, and offline reward model are insufficient for reliable cross-domain policy improvement.

AI.tldr: Trajectory mining reveals readable GUI skills but fails to reliably improve cross-domain policies using current methods.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether explicit skill libraries can be mined from interaction data to improve downstream policies in GUI agents.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: segment GUI trajectories, cluster segments into candidate skills, train skill-aware policy from resulting annotations (using GRPO).
AI.result: Mined clusters are readable (5/8 clusters have ≥0.95 purity against InteraSkill Workflows labels), but skill transfer is poor: minimal accuracy improvements on benchmarks (18.5%→20.5% on IW, no change on BrowseComp+), underperforming frequency priors.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory mining can expose inspectable skill structure, but current boundary detector, orderless segment representation, and offline reward model are insufficient for reliable cross-domain policy improvement. The study serves as a diagnostic rather than a solution.

title: Matching Markets meet Cumulative Prospect Theory: Towards Optimal and Adversarially Robust Learning
summary: We study a multi-agent multi-armed bandit problem in the competitive setup with two-sided matching markets under a human centric decision making model. To capture human preferences, we use cumulative prospect theory (CPT) that weighs the actions of the agent in a nonlinear fashion using a ($α$-Hölder continuous) weight function. CPT has been widely used in behavioral economics and risk sensitive machine learning to emulate human preferences. We analyze the state-of-the-art learning algorithm with CPT weight distorted rewards and obtain a player optimal regret of $\mathcal{O}(K\log T \left(\frac{1}Δ\right)^{2/α})$, where $K$ denotes the number of arms, $T$ is the learning horizon, and $Δ$ represents (suitably defined) players' minimum preference gap. Noticing the dependence on $Δ$ to be sub-optimal, we further improve this regret by judiciously selecting the active set of arms during exploration, which removes the dependence on $K$ in the dominant term and achieves an improved (optimal) regret guarantees in the setting where the number of arms $K$ is significantly larger than the number of players $N$. In addition, we consider adversarial markets where the observed rewards of the agents may be corrupted. We propose and analyze algorithms for robust markets with CPT as risk sensitive measure in both settings where the total corruption budget is known and where it is unknown, and establish logarithmic player-optimal regret guarantees in both cases.

AI.tldr: This paper improves regret bounds for multi-agent bandits in competitive matching markets using CPT for human preferences, removing K dependence and handling adversarial corruption.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to study multi-agent multi-armed bandit problems in competitive two-sided matching markets under human-centric decision making, capturing human preferences via Cumulative Prospect Theory (CPT). The goal is to address suboptimal dependence on the preference gap Δ and handle adversarial corruption in observed rewards.
AI.method: The method involves analyzing a state-of-the-art learning algorithm with CPT weight distorted rewards, then improving it by judiciously selecting active sets of arms during exploration. For adversarial markets, algorithms are proposed and analyzed for both known and unknown total corruption budgets.
AI.result: The paper obtains a player optimal regret of O(K log T (1/Δ)^{2/α}) initially, then improves this by removing dependence on K in the dominant term, achieving optimal regret when K >> N. For adversarial markets with corruption, it establishes logarithmic player-optimal regret guarantees for both known and unknown corruption budgets.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the proposed algorithms achieve player-optimal regret guarantees for multi-agent multi-armed bandit problems in competitive two-sided matching markets under CPT-based human-centric decision making. It demonstrates improved regret bounds by removing dependence on K and handling adversarial corruption, with both known and unknown corruption budgets.

title: Rethinking Shrinkage Bias in LLM FP4 Pretraining: Geometric Origin, Systemic Impact, and UFP4 Recipe
summary: FP4 training promises substantial reductions in memory and computation cost for LLM pretraining, yet current FP4 hardware paths and recipes, including NVIDIA Blackwell/Rubin-class systems and AMD MI350-series GPUs, remain centered on E2M1 data elements. In this study, we identify a fundamental limitation of that choice: non-uniform formats such as E2M1 inherently suffer from Shrinkage Bias, a systematic negative rounding error caused by the geometric asymmetry of their representable bins. We show that this bias accumulates multiplicatively across layers and is amplified by the Random Hadamard Transform (RHT), providing a unified explanation for the training instability observed in existing E2M1-based FP4 recipes. In contrast, uniform grids (E1M2/INT4) bypass this grid-geometry error and better convert the improved bucket utilization from RHT into higher quantization quality. Based on this finding, we propose UFP4, a uniform 4-bit training recipe that applies RHT to all three training GEMMs while restricting stochastic rounding to dY alone. On Dense 1.5B, MoE 7.9B, and MoE 124B long-run pretraining, UFP4 consistently achieves lower BF16-relative loss degradation than strong E2M1-based baselines, supported by scaling-law analysis and ablation studies. Our results suggest that future accelerators should support E1M2/INT4-style uniform 4-bit grids as first-class training primitives alongside E2M1.

AI.tldr: UFP4 proposes using uniform 4-bit formats (E1M2/INT4) instead of non-uniform E2M1 for LLM pretraining, addressing Shrinkage Bias in existing FP4 approaches through Random Hadamard Transform and selective stochastic rounding.
AI.motivation: Current FP4 training approaches using E2M1 formats suffer from Shrinkage Bias - systematic negative rounding errors due to geometric asymmetry of representable bins, causing training instability that accumulates across layers.
AI.method: UFP4 uses uniform 4-bit formats (E1M2/INT4) with Random Hadamard Transform applied to all three training GEMMs, restricting stochastic rounding to dY alone to maintain training stability.
AI.result: UFP4 consistently achieves lower BF16-relative loss degradation than E2M1-based baselines across Dense 1.5B, MoE 7.9B, and MoE 124B pretraining, validated by scaling-law analysis and ablation studies.
AI.conclusion: Future accelerators should support uniform 4-bit grids (E1M2/INT4) as first-class training primitives alongside E2M1, as uniform formats bypass grid-geometry errors and better utilize Random Hadamard Transform benefits.

title: SL-S4Wave: Self-Supervised Learning of Physiological Waveforms with Structured State Space Models
summary: Modeling long-sequence medical time series data, such as electrocardiograms (ECG), poses significant challenges due to high sampling rates, multichannel signal complexity, inherent noise, and limited labeled data. While recent self-supervised learning (SSL) methods, based on various encoder architectures such as convolutional neural networks, have been proposed to learn representations from unlabeled data, they often fall short in capturing long-range dependencies and noise-invariant features. Structured state space models (S4) excel at long-sequence modeling, but existing S4 architectures fail to capture the unique characteristics of multichannel physiological waveforms. In this work, we propose SL-S4Wave, a self-supervised learning framework that combines contrastive learning with a tailored encoder built on structured state space models. The encoder incorporates multi-layer global convolution using multiscale subkernels, enabling the capture of both fine-grained local patterns and long-range temporal dependencies in noisy, high-resolution multichannel waveforms. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that SL-S4Wave (1) consistently outperforms state-of-the-art supervised and self-supervised baselines in a challenging arrhythmia detection task, (2) achieves high performance with significantly fewer labeled examples, showcasing strong label efficiency, and (3) maintains robust performance on long waveform segments, highlighting its capacity to model complex temporal dynamics in long sequences that most existing approaches fail to efficiently model, and (4) transfers effectively to unseen arrhythmia types, underscoring its robust cross-domain generalization. We additionally evaluate SL-S4Wave on multiple EEG tasks, achieving superior performance over strong baselines, demonstrating generalizability of our approach beyond cardiac waveforms.

AI.tldr: SL-S4Wave is a self-supervised learning framework combining contrastive learning with a tailored structured state space model encoder for long-sequence medical time series analysis, achieving state-of-the-art performance on arrhythmia detection and EEG tasks with strong label efficiency and generalization.
AI.motivation: Existing self-supervised learning methods for medical time series often fail to capture long-range dependencies and noise-invariant features, while structured state space models (S4) excel at long-sequence modeling but don't account for multichannel physiological waveform characteristics.
AI.method: SL-S4Wave combines contrastive learning with a tailored encoder built on structured state space models (S4). The encoder incorporates multi-layer global convolution using multiscale subkernels to capture both fine-grained local patterns and long-range temporal dependencies in noisy, high-resolution multichannel waveforms.
AI.result: SL-S4Wave consistently outperforms state-of-the-art supervised and self-supervised baselines in arrhythmia detection, achieves high performance with significantly fewer labeled examples (strong label efficiency), maintains robust performance on long waveform segments, transfers effectively to unseen arrhythmia types, and generalizes well to EEG tasks beyond cardiac waveforms.
AI.conclusion: SL-S4Wave effectively addresses key challenges in modeling long-sequence medical time series by capturing both local patterns and long-range dependencies while being noise-invariant, achieving superior performance with limited labeled data and demonstrating strong generalization capabilities across different physiological signals.

title: Interpretable Sperm Morphology Classification via Attention-Guided Deep Learning
summary: Male infertility is a major cause of couple infertility, often linked to abnormal sperm morphology. While deep learning models offer automated analysis, most lack interpretability, limiting their clinical adoption. This study proposes an attention-guided deep learning framework for sperm morphology classification. We combine a pretrained EfficientNet-B0 with a Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) to focus on key areas of the sperm head, improving both accuracy and interpretability. Evaluated on the SMIDS and HuSHem public datasets, our model achieves accuracies of 90.2% and 93.9% (macro F1 scores of 0.913 and 0.948), outperforming SimpleCNN and standard EfficientNet-B0. Furthermore, we use Grad-CAM++ visualizations to highlight features influencing the model's decisions. The results demonstrate that this accurate and transparent framework is a practical tool for automated sperm analysis in fertility clinics.

AI.tldr: Proposed attention-guided deep learning framework using EfficientNet-B0 with CBAM for sperm morphology classification, achieving high accuracy and interpretability for clinical use.
AI.motivation: Male infertility often linked to abnormal sperm morphology. While deep learning models offer automated analysis, most lack interpretability, limiting clinical adoption.
AI.method: Combined pretrained EfficientNet-B0 with Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) to focus on key areas of sperm head for improved accuracy and interpretability.
AI.result: Achieved accuracies of 90.2% and 93.9% (macro F1 scores of 0.913 and 0.948) on SMIDS and HuSHem datasets, outperforming SimpleCNN and standard EfficientNet-B0. Used Grad-CAM++ visualizations for model interpretability.
AI.conclusion: The accurate and transparent framework demonstrates potential as a practical tool for automated sperm analysis in fertility clinics.

title: Adversarial Bandit Optimization with Globally Bounded Perturbations to Convex Losses
summary:
We study adversarial bandit optimization in which the loss functions may be non-convex and non-smooth. In each round, the learner selects an action and observes only the loss incurred at that action. The loss consists of an underlying convex and $β$-smooth component and an adversarial perturbation that may be chosen after observing the learner's action. The perturbations are subject to a global budget controlling their cumulative magnitude over time.
  This framework extends the globally budgeted, post-action perturbation model from underlying linear losses to general convex and $β$-smooth losses. For this broader class, we establish expected regret guarantees that explicitly characterize the effect of the perturbation budget.
  To establish these guarantees, we modify a standard bandit optimization algorithm and develop an analysis that controls the additional regret caused by the perturbations. In the absence of perturbations, our results reduce to regret guarantees for the standard bandit convex optimization setting with $β$-smooth losses.

AI.tldr: This paper studies adversarial bandit optimization with non-convex/non-smooth losses, where losses consist of convex smooth components plus adversarial perturbations subject to a global budget, and establishes regret bounds that account for the perturbation budget.
AI.motivation: To extend the globally budgeted, post-action perturbation model from linear losses to more general convex and smooth losses, enabling analysis of adversarial bandit optimization in more realistic settings where losses may have both structured components and adversarial elements.
AI.method: Modified a standard bandit optimization algorithm and developed a novel analysis technique that controls the additional regret caused by adversarial perturbations, specifically handling the interaction between the convex smooth component and bounded adversarial perturbations.
AI.result: Established expected regret guarantees that explicitly characterize the effect of the perturbation budget on performance, with results reducing to standard bandit convex optimization regret bounds when perturbations are absent.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully extends perturbation-budgeted adversarial bandit optimization beyond linear losses to convex smooth losses, providing theoretical guarantees that quantify how perturbation budgets affect regret in more general settings.

title: Context-Aware Hierarchical Bayesian Modeling of IVF Laboratory Environmental Conditions
summary: IVF pregnancy rates are routinely modeled using patient-level variables, while high-resolution laboratory environmental data remain underutilized. We show that this is a missed opportunity. Rather than relying on raw sensor averages, we engineer 55 context-aware temporal features, including rolling thermal stability, simultaneous temperature-humidity adherence, peak stress duration, and post-stress recovery speed, that capture the dynamics of incubator microenvironments. On 61 weeks of data from an Asian IVF clinic, these features reduce cross-validated prediction error to 1.27%, compared to 3-5% for raw averages. We then train a hierarchical Bayesian Beta regression model that shares environmental effects across an Asian and a Northern European clinic via partial pooling, while preserving site-specific baselines. On held-out data from the Northern European clinic, the model achieves R2 = 0.86 and a 64% error reduction for the 35-39 age group over a naive baseline, demonstrating that structured environmental monitoring contains clinically meaningful, transferable signal.

AI.tldr: Engineered context-aware temporal features from incubator microenvironments improve IVF pregnancy rate prediction by reducing error to 1.27% vs 3-5% for raw averages. A hierarchical Bayesian model transfers environmental effects across clinics while preserving site-specific baselines.
AI.motivation: IVF pregnancy rates are typically modeled using patient-level variables, while high-resolution laboratory environmental data remain underutilized, representing a missed opportunity for improved prediction.
AI.method: Engineered 55 context-aware temporal features capturing incubator microenvironment dynamics (rolling thermal stability, simultaneous temperature-humidity adherence, peak stress duration, post-stress recovery speed). Trained hierarchical Bayesian Beta regression model using partial pooling to share environmental effects across Asian and Northern European clinics while preserving site-specific baselines.
AI.result: On 61 weeks of data from an Asian IVF clinic, features reduced cross-validated prediction error to 1.27% vs 3-5% for raw averages. On held-out data from Northern European clinic, model achieved R² = 0.86 and 64% error reduction for 35-39 age group over naive baseline.
AI.conclusion: Structured environmental monitoring contains clinically meaningful, transferable signal that can significantly improve IVF pregnancy rate prediction across different clinics and settings.

title: Score Approximation for Diffusion Models on Arbitrary Low-Dimensional Structures
summary: The remarkable success of score-based diffusion models has spurred significant efforts to establish their theoretical foundations. However, existing complexity bounds for score approximation rely heavily on restrictive assumptions like Lipschitz continuous densities or smooth manifold supports, which are routinely violated by the singularities, sharp boundaries, and disjoint clusters inherent to real-world perceptual data. This work establishes a universal score approximation theorem that works for any distribution supported on any compact set of upper Minkowski dimension $d$. Using a novel discrete-mixture formulation, we prove that the score function can be approximated with a ReLU network whose complexity grows exponentially only with $d$, thus breaking the exponential curse of ambient dimensionality. Combined with existing theories on accurately solving the backward diffusion SDE for arbitrary compact distributions, our work shows that diffusion models readily adapt to irregular, non-smooth data structures, explaining their competence in real-world generative tasks.

AI.tldr: Breakthrough: Universal score approximation theorem works for any compact distribution using discrete-mixture formulation, breaking exponential curse of ambient dimensionality.
AI.motivation: Existing complexity bounds for score approximation rely on restrictive assumptions (Lipschitz continuous densities, smooth manifold supports) that don't match real-world data which has singularities, sharp boundaries, and disjoint clusters.
AI.method: Use a novel discrete-mixture formulation to prove that score functions can be approximated with ReLU networks whose complexity grows exponentially only with intrinsic dimension (Minkowski dimension d), not ambient dimension.
AI.result: Established universal score approximation theorem for any distribution on any compact set of upper Minkowski dimension d. Score can be approximated with ReLU network with complexity exponential only in d, not ambient dimension.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion models can readily adapt to irregular, non-smooth data structures, explaining their competence in real-world generative tasks when combined with existing backward diffusion SDE theories.

title: What Do Safety-Aligned LLMs Learn From Mixed Compliance Demonstrations?
summary: Prior work has shown that in-context demonstrations can jailbreak language models, but it remains unclear how models interpret different types of compliance demonstrations. We study this by mixing benign compliance demonstrations (non-harmful request, helpful response) with harmful compliance demonstrations (harmful request, helpful response) and testing three hypotheses about how demonstration composition drives harmful compliance. Across four models, we find that benign and harmful demonstrations are not interchangeable: benign demonstrations can either reduce or increase harmful compliance depending on the model. We further show that preference optimization is the critical training stage that prevents benign demonstrations from increasing harmful compliance, that demonstration ordering exhibits strong recency bias, and that models differ in how refusal interacts with in-context learning: some adopt demonstrated formatting even when refusing, while others override all in-context signals upon refusal. Taken together, this work moves beyond showing that demonstration-based jailbreaking works to characterizing how it works: what models extract from compliance demonstrations depends on demonstration content, ordering, and training methodology.

AI.tldr: In-context demonstrations impact language models' harmful compliance differently based on content, ordering, and training. Benign demonstrations don't directly substitute for harmful ones and can either reduce or increase compliance depending on the model, with preference optimization being key to preventing benign demonstrations from increasing harmful compliance.
AI.motivation: To understand how language models interpret and respond to different types of compliance demonstrations during in-context learning, specifically examining how mixing benign and harmful demonstrations affects models' willingness to comply with harmful requests.
AI.method: Mixed benign compliance demonstrations (non-harmful request with helpful response) with harmful compliance demonstrations (harmful request with helpful response), testing three hypotheses about how demonstration composition drives harmful compliance. Tested across four different language models.
AI.result:
1. Benign and harmful demonstrations are not interchangeable - benign demonstrations can either reduce or increase harmful compliance depending on the model.
2. Preference optimization training is critical for preventing benign demonstrations from increasing harmful compliance.
3. Strong recency bias in demonstration ordering.
4. Models differ in how refusal interacts with in-context learning: some adopt demonstrated formatting even when refusing, while others override all in-context signals upon refusal.
AI.conclusion: Demonstration-based jailbreaking effectiveness depends on demonstration content, ordering, and training methodology rather than just presence of demonstrations. The study moves beyond simply showing that demonstration-based jailbreaking works to characterizing how it works in practice.

title: ADaPT: Token-Level Decoupling for Efficient Large Reasoning Models
summary: Large reasoning models rely on long chain-of-thought to achieve strong performance, but applying such reasoning uniformly incurs high computational cost. Existing efficiency-oriented methods attempt to shorten or mix reasoning strategies, yet often degrade reasoning capability. We identify the root cause as sequence-level coupling between efficiency incentives and correctness optimization, which implicitly penalizes long but correct reasoning trajectories. To address this issue, we propose Adaptive Dual-Process Thinking (ADaPT), a token-level dual-process framework that explicitly decouples efficiency and correctness signals during training. ADaPT introduces a mode-selection token to control fast and slow reasoning, applying efficiency-related rewards exclusively to this token to avoid penalizing correct long reasoning while encouraging efficiency when appropriate. Moreover, ADaPT enables precise and continuous control over the efficiency-performance trade-off at inference time: by adjusting the generation probability of the mode-selection token, a single trained model can smoothly move along the efficiency-performance Pareto frontier. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ADaPT significantly reduces inference cost while maintaining strong reasoning performance across multiple benchmarks.

AI.tldr: ADaPT is a token-level dual-process framework that decouples efficiency and correctness signals during training, enabling adaptive trade-off between reasoning performance and computational cost through mode-selection tokens.
AI.motivation: Current large reasoning models rely on long chain-of-thought reasoning which incurs high computational cost, but existing efficiency methods degrade reasoning capability by penalizing correct long reasoning trajectories through sequence-level coupling between efficiency and correctness optimization.
AI.method: ADaPT introduces token-level dual-process thinking with a mode-selection token that controls fast and slow reasoning modes. It applies efficiency rewards exclusively to this token during training to avoid penalizing correct long reasoning while encouraging efficiency when appropriate.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show ADaPT significantly reduces inference cost while maintaining strong reasoning performance across multiple benchmarks, enabling precise continuous control over efficiency-performance trade-off at inference time.
AI.conclusion: ADaPT effectively addresses the sequence-level coupling problem in reasoning efficiency optimization, providing a practical solution for balancing computational cost and reasoning performance through explicit decoupling of efficiency and correctness signals.

title: Multi-LCB: Extending LiveCodeBench to Multiple Programming Languages
summary:
LiveCodeBench (LCB) has recently become a widely adopted benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) on code-generation tasks. By curating competitive programming problems, constantly adding fresh problems to the set, and filtering them by release dates, LCB provides contamination-aware evaluation and offers a holistic view of coding capability. However, LCB remains restricted to Python, leaving open the question of whether LLMs can generalize across the diverse programming languages required in real-world software engineering.
  We introduce Multi-LCB, a benchmark for evaluating LLMs across twelve programming languages, including Python. Multi-LCB transforms Python tasks from the LCB dataset into equivalent tasks in other languages while preserving LCB's contamination controls and evaluation protocol. Because it is fully compatible with the original LCB format, Multi-LCB will automatically track future LCB updates, enabling systematic assessment of cross-language code generation competence and requiring models to sustain performance well beyond Python.
  We evaluated 24 LLMs for instruction and reasoning on Multi-LCB, uncovering evidence of Python overfitting, language-specific contamination, and substantial disparities in multilingual performance. Our results establish Multi-LCB as a rigorous new benchmark for multi-programming-language code evaluation, directly addressing LCB's primary limitation and exposing critical gaps in current LLM capabilities.

AI.tldr: Multi-LCB extends LiveCodeBench (LCB) to evaluate LLMs across 12 programming languages, not just Python, exposing limitations like Python overfitting and revealing substantial performance disparities across languages.
AI.motivation: LCB is widely used but limited to Python, while real-world software engineering requires diverse programming languages. The authors aim to assess whether LLMs can generalize across multiple languages.
AI.method: Multi-LCB transforms Python tasks from LCB into equivalent tasks in 11 other programming languages while preserving LCB's contamination controls and evaluation protocol. It's fully compatible with the original LCB format and will automatically track future LCB updates.
AI.result: Evaluation of 24 LLMs revealed Python overfitting, language-specific contamination, and substantial disparities in multilingual performance across different programming languages.
AI.conclusion: Multi-LCB addresses LCB's primary limitation by enabling systematic assessment of cross-language code generation, exposing critical gaps in current LLM capabilities for multi-programming-language tasks.

title: Compositionality Emerges in a Narrow Depth-Connectivity Regime: Architecture Constraints and Solution Manifolds
summary: Compositionality is believed to be the foundation for generalization, enabling models to reuse meaningful primitives in novel combinations. Yet, models trained with standard gradient-based optimization rarely, and often only weakly, exhibit compositional internal structure, and it remains unclear how or why such compositionality forms. In this work, we show that compositionality emerges in a narrow connectivity-depth sweet spot. Along the connectivity axis, compositionality only appears in some specifically sparse networks, heavily depends on which connections remain rather than on weights' sparsity alone. Along the depth axis, compositionality emerges within a narrow, target-dependent regime, peaking at specific depths, while both shallower and deeper networks fail. When either the depth or connectivity condition is violated, gradient descent silently converges to fractured solutions rather than compositional ones. To discover and exploit this emergence, we introduce (i) similarity-based pruning (SP) to recover compositional connectivity and (ii) a heuristic depth predictor to estimate where compositionality is most likely to appear. Finally, we support these empirical findings with a theoretical framework based on compositional sparsity, volume-ratio arguments, and feature-interference bounds, explaining why compositional solutions are reachable only in a narrow depth-connectivity regime.

AI.tldr: Compositionality emerges in neural networks only within a narrow connectivity-depth sweet spot, requiring specific sparsity patterns rather than just weight sparsity, and peaking at target-dependent optimal depths.
AI.motivation: Despite compositionality being fundamental for generalization, standard gradient-based optimization rarely produces networks with clear compositional internal structure, and it's unclear how such compositionality forms.
AI.method: The authors investigate connectivity-depth relationship for compositionality, introduce similarity-based pruning to recover compositional connectivity, develop a heuristic depth predictor, and provide theoretical framework based on compositional sparsity and feature-interference bounds.
AI.result: Compositionality only appears in specific sparse networks (depending on which connections remain), peaks at target-dependent optimal depths, and requires both connectivity and depth conditions. When conditions are violated, gradient descent converges to fractured solutions.
AI.conclusion: Compositionality emerges in a narrow connectivity-depth sweet spot, requiring both specific sparsity patterns and target-dependent optimal depths. Theoretical framework explains why compositional solutions are only reachable in this narrow regime.

title: FlowEdit: Associative Memory for Lifelong Pronunciation Adaptation in Flow-Matching TTS
summary: Flow-matching text-to-speech systems achieve remarkable zero-shot quality but remain static after deployment: pronunciation errors on out-of-vocabulary proper nouns persist unless the model is retrained. We introduce FlowEdit, a life-long adaptation framework for frozen flow-matching TTS that learns pronunciation corrections as latent conditioning edits rather than weight updates. When corrective feedback is provided, FlowEdit optimizes a token-level perturbation in the text embedding space, then stores the correction in a Modern Hopfield Network serving as content-addressable episodic memory. At inference, corrections are retrieved via soft attention with a similarity gate, enabling fuzzy morphological matching. On our curated benchmark of 312 multilingual proper nouns across 18 language families, FlowEdit reduces target-word Phoneme Error Rate by 92.7% relative to the zero-shot baseline while maintaining identical general-speech quality. Corrections complete in approximately 15 seconds on a single GPU.

AI.tldr: FlowEdit enables lifelong adaptation of flow-matching TTS models by learning and storing pronunciation corrections as latent embeddings, eliminating the need for model retraining.
AI.motivation: Flow-matching TTS systems achieve great zero-shot quality but remain static after deployment - pronunciation errors on proper nouns persist unless the entire model is retrained, which is inefficient and impractical.
AI.method: When corrective feedback is provided, FlowEdit optimizes token-level perturbations in text embedding space and stores corrections in a Modern Hopfield Network serving as content-addressable episodic memory. At inference, corrections are retrieved via soft attention with similarity gating.
AI.result: On a benchmark of 312 multilingual proper nouns across 18 language families, FlowEdit reduces target-word Phoneme Error Rate by 92.7% relative to zero-shot baseline while maintaining identical general-speech quality. Corrections complete in ~15 seconds on a single GPU.
AI.conclusion: FlowEdit provides an efficient framework for lifelong adaptation of frozen flow-matching TTS models, enabling pronunciation corrections without weight updates or retraining, making TTS systems more adaptable and maintainable in production.

title: Towards Graph-Based Deep Learning for Map Generalization: Insights from Building Footprints Simplification and Aggregation
summary: Map generalization remains one of the fundamental tasks in cartography, especially for the simplification and aggregation of complex building footprints. This study presents the first exploratory application of graph-based deep learning to both tasks, reformulating simplification as node movement prediction and aggregation as link prediction within a unified graph learning framework. We evaluate representative graph neural network architectures (GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE) on multi-scale building datasets, showing that GraphSAGE demonstrates relative strengths in link prediction accuracy, while also revealing persistent challenges in precise node movement prediction.
