To provide formal guarantees about NN behavior, many verification methods rely on optimizable linear relaxations of activation functions. However, existing techniques depend on hand-crafted relaxations for each activation function. Extension to state-of-the-art activation functions therefore requires substantial manual effort. In contrast, our approach SLiR (Shifting-based Linear Relaxations) is broadly applicable, requiring only a Lipschitz constant or a set of critical points. SLiR parameterizes relaxations by their slope and computes the corresponding offset via a shifting procedure that ensures sound upper and lower bounds over the input domain, enabling efficient optimization while maintaining correctness. Our experiments show that SLiR produces tight relaxations across a wide range of practical activation functions and enables verification of up to 7.8x more properties compared to state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: SLiR is a broadly applicable method for generating optimizable linear relaxations of neural network activation functions, requiring only minimal information and outperforming existing verification techniques.
AI.motivation: Existing verification methods rely on hand-crafted relaxations for each activation function, requiring substantial manual effort and limiting applicability to new, state-of-the-art activation functions.
AI.method: SLiR parameterizes relaxations by slope and computes offsets via a shifting procedure that ensures sound upper and lower bounds over the input domain, requiring only a Lipschitz constant or set of critical points.
AI.result: SLiR produces tight relaxations across a wide range of practical activation functions and enables verification of up to 7.8x more properties compared to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: SLiR provides a broadly applicable, efficient, and tight relaxation method for neural network verification, significantly outperforming existing techniques.

title: Recurrent neural networks approximate continuous functions
summary: Classical approximation theorems ask for a new neural network whenever the target accuracy is improved. This paper studies the opposite possibility: can the network be chosen once and for all, and can accuracy be bought only by letting it run longer? We prove that this is possible for every continuous function on [-1,1]. More precisely, each such function is uniformly approximated by the time evolution of a single ReLU recurrent neural network with fixed weights and fixed hidden dimension. The mechanism behind the construction is a new intermediate model, the Turing machine with neural units (TMNU). This model retains the algorithmic freedom needed to implement polynomial approximation schemes, while remaining rigid enough to be simulated by RNNs with explicit bounds on hidden dimension and weight magnitude. The resulting convergence rates reflect the underlying polynomial approximation rates. We complement the construction with minimax lower bounds showing that runtime is not merely a proof artifact, but an unavoidable resource in this fixed-network approximation paradigm.

AI.tldr: A single fixed ReLU RNN can approximate any continuous function on [-1,1] by running longer, without changing weights or architecture.
AI.motivation: Classical neural network approximation requires new networks for better accuracy. This paper investigates whether fixed networks can improve accuracy through longer runtime instead of architectural changes.
AI.method: Introduces Turing Machine with Neural Units (TMNU) as an intermediate model to maintain algorithmic flexibility while being simulatable by ReLU RNNs with fixed weights and hidden dimension. Uses polynomial approximation schemes.
AI.result: Proves any continuous function on [-1,1] can be uniformly approximated by the time evolution of a single fixed ReLU RNN. Achieves convergence rates matching underlying polynomial approximation rates. Shows runtime is an essential resource via minimax lower bounds.
AI.conclusion: Fixed-network approximation is possible where accuracy improves with runtime rather than architectural changes, introducing a new paradigm for neural network approximation.

title: Quantum-classical physics-informed Kolmogorov-Arnold networks for PDEs
summary: We develop QCPIKAN, the first quantum-classical physics-informed Kolmogorov-Arnold network designed to solve partial differential equations (PDEs). Built upon Chebyshev-polynomial KAN layers and parameterized quantum circuits, this hybrid framework embeds physical constraints into the training loss to enforce physical consistency. Our theoretical investigations grounded in approximation theory prove that this design accelerates high-frequency error convergence to an exponential rate and effectively mitigates numerical dispersion. We validate the framework across three typical seepage scenarios in porous media, including single-phase flow, component transport and two-phase flow. Compared with existing quantum-classical physics-informed neural networks, QCPIKAN achieves superior performance in global prediction accuracy, local error control, dynamic evolution tracking and displacement front localization. This work provides a robust and efficient alternative for solving complex PDEs.

AI.tldr: QCPIKAN is a quantum-classical physics-informed neural network using Kolmogorov-Arnold architecture with Chebyshev-polynomial layers and quantum circuits to solve PDEs with exponential high-frequency convergence and reduced dispersion.
AI.motivation: To develop a hybrid quantum-classical framework for solving partial differential equations that overcomes limitations of existing quantum-classical physics-informed neural networks, particularly in accuracy, error control, and physical consistency.
AI.method: Built QCPIKAN using Chebyshev-polynomial Kolmogorov-Arnold network layers combined with parameterized quantum circuits, embedding physical constraints into the training loss to enforce physical consistency.
AI.result: Validated across three seepage scenarios (single-phase flow, component transport, two-phase flow), achieving superior performance in global accuracy, local error control, dynamic tracking, and displacement front localization compared to existing quantum-classical PINNs.
AI.conclusion: QCPIKAN provides a robust and efficient alternative for solving complex PDEs, demonstrating accelerated high-frequency error convergence and effective mitigation of numerical dispersion through theoretical and empirical validation.

title: Constrained hybrid modelling to predict microbial dynamics and organic matter turnover in soil systems
summary: Soil microorganisms control organic matter cycling and largely determine how soil systems can cope with and mitigate climate change and environmental threats. Representing microbial dynamics in process-based soil models is therefore critical to predict carbon cycling in soils, albeit highly challenging to inform from data. One promising approach to improve their parametrisation is the integration of genomic data, yet modelling the complex and unknown relationship between genomes and the processes the microbes are driving is an unsolved problem. In this work, we present the first hybrid modeling framework for deriving biokinetic parameter values of a process-based soil organic matter turnover model from metagenome-inferred functional traits based on DNA sequencing data. Our model predicts biokinetic parameters of the process-based model from genomic trait data with a neural network and integrates constraints from ecological theory and literature to ensure realistic behavior, even of non-observed state variables. We evaluate our method on synthetic genomic trait datasets of varying complexity and on real data, showing that our approach improves performance over multiple baselines and learns the dynamics of unmeasurable components of the process-based model effectively, even for small training datasets.

AI.tldr: A hybrid framework that uses neural networks to predict soil organic matter model parameters from metagenomic data, integrating ecological constraints for better carbon cycling predictions.
AI.motivation: Soil microbes are crucial for organic matter cycling and climate change mitigation, but representing microbial dynamics in soil models is challenging due to complex genome-process relationships and limited data.
AI.method: A hybrid modeling framework combining: 1) neural networks to predict biokinetic parameters of soil organic matter models from metagenomic functional traits, 2) integration of ecological theory and literature constraints to ensure realistic behavior of both observed and unobserved state variables.
AI.result: The method improves performance over multiple baselines on synthetic and real genomic datasets, effectively learns dynamics of unmeasurable model components, and works well even with small training datasets.
AI.conclusion: This first hybrid framework successfully integrates genomic data with process-based soil modeling, enabling better prediction of soil carbon cycling by linking microbial functional traits to ecosystem processes through machine learning.

title: Critical Percolation as a Synthetic Data Model for Interpretability
comment: 21 pages, 10 figures, accepted to the Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop at ICML 2026
summary: Neural networks learn features that reflect the hierarchical, multi-scale structure of natural data. Synthetic datasets used to evaluate interpretability methods typically lack this structure, limiting their value as realistic toy models. To close this gap, we introduce a family of synthetic datasets consisting of hierarchical functions defined on critical mean-field percolation clusters embedded in a high-dimensional data space. The percolation data consists of sparse, low-dimensional fractal clusters with a power-law size distribution. Latent variables modeling a taxonomic hierarchy generate each data point's target value. The data model is analytically tractable with known critical exponents that fix its properties without requiring hyperparameter tuning. We leverage a mapping between percolation clusters, random trees, and additive coalescence to propose an almost linear-time algorithm to jointly sample a random tree and its hierarchical latent decomposition, enabling data generation at arbitrary scale. Using probing experiments, we find that the model's ground-truth latent variables can be linearly decoded from neural network activations. Together, sparsity, self-similarity, power-law statistics, and analytical tractability make critical percolation a principled testbed for interpretability research.

AI.tldr: Researchers introduce hierarchical synthetic datasets based on critical mean-field percolation clusters to create realistic toy models for evaluating interpretability methods.
AI.motivation: Existing synthetic datasets lack the hierarchical, multi-scale structure of natural data, limiting their usefulness for testing interpretability methods. A more principled testbed with realistic properties is needed.
AI.method: Created a family of synthetic datasets using hierarchical functions defined on critical mean-field percolation clusters embedded in high-dimensional space. These clusters are sparse, low-dimensional fractals with power-law size distributions. Latent variables model taxonomic hierarchies to generate target values. Used mapping between percolation clusters, random trees, and additive coalescence to develop an almost linear-time algorithm for sampling.
AI.result: Ground-truth latent variables can be linearly decoded from neural network activations, demonstrating the dataset's utility for interpretability research. The model is analytically tractable with known critical exponents that fix properties without hyperparameter tuning.
AI.conclusion: Critical percolation provides a principled testbed for interpretability research due to its sparsity, self-similarity, power-law statistics, and analytical tractability, enabling realistic evaluation of interpretability methods.

title: On the Variance of Temporal Difference Learning and its Reduction Using Control Variates
summary: We analyze the variance of temporal difference (TD) learning using the phased setting with tabular representation, and show that one of the mechanisms behind its ability to reduce variance is by effectively aggregating over a larger number of independent trajectories. Based on this insight, we demonstrate that (1) the variance of TD is asymptotically bounded from above by Monte Carlo (MC) estimators, and (2) shorter horizon updates incurs less variance for a fixed number of samples. Beyond TD, we show that Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE), a method for estimating the advantage function, can be seen as a type of regression-adjusted control variate, which achieves a tighter bound on the variance compared to TD in the large-sample limit. Finally, we numerically illustrate the behaviors of these estimators with carefully designed environments.

AI.tldr: TD learning reduces variance by aggregating more independent trajectories; DAE uses regression-adjusted control variates for even tighter variance bounds.
AI.motivation: To understand variance properties of TD learning compared to Monte Carlo methods, and explore variance reduction mechanisms in temporal difference and advantage estimation methods.
AI.method: Analyze TD learning variance using phased setting with tabular representation; demonstrate TD variance bounds vs MC; analyze Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE) as regression-adjusted control variate; numerical experiments with designed environments.
AI.result: (1) TD variance asymptotically bounded above by MC estimators; (2) shorter horizon updates yield less variance for fixed samples; (3) DAE achieves tighter variance bound than TD in large-sample limit.
AI.conclusion: TD learning reduces variance by aggregating independent trajectories; DAE provides superior variance reduction via regression-adjusted control variates.

title: Train, Retrieve, or Both? A Four-Arm Head-to-Head for Correct Statutory Citation on the Ontario Residential Tenancies Act
summary: Self-represented tenants, landlords, and help-desk staff need to be pointed at the provision of law that actually governs a question, with a correct statutory citation. We study this task on the Ontario Residential Tenancies Act, 2006 (RTA) and its core regulation, asking the operator's question empirically: is fine-tuning enough, or is hybrid retrieval needed? We run a four-arm head-to-head on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct (base zero-shot, LoRA SFT-only, RAG-only, and an SFT+RAG hybrid), scored on citation exact-match (section+subsection) over a small, human-verification-pending real eval set. The base model cannot cite the RTA and SFT-only mis-recalls sections; retrieval is essential and drives hallucination to zero by construction; and the SFT+RAG hybrid scores highest at 0.481 exact-match with zero hallucinated citations. Its edge comes from SFT making provision selection more robust to the higher-recall candidate sets that hurt zero-shot RAG. Notably, this cheap bge-small hybrid matches or beats a pipeline built on bigger, specialized retrieval models (a larger embedder and a cross-encoder reranker), and a larger/improved training set does not help either: strong statutory-citation performance here does not require specialized retrieval models or more data. The artifact zeroes hallucination and clears the lift-over-base bar but does not reach the aspirational 0.70 exact-match target. All results are on a small, human-verification-pending real eval set and are reported as preliminary.

AI.tldr: Hybrid SFT+RAG outperforms other methods for statutory citation in the Ontario RTA, achieving the best exact-match score without hallucinations, and shows specialized retrieval models or more data aren't needed.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the need for accurate statutory citation in legal contexts, specifically for self-represented tenants, landlords, and help-desk staff under the Ontario Residential Tenancies Act, 2006 (RTA). The study aims to determine whether fine-tuning alone is sufficient or if hybrid retrieval is necessary for this task.
AI.method: The method involves a four-arm head-to-head comparison using Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct: base zero-shot, LoRA SFT-only, RAG-only, and an SFT+RAG hybrid. Performance is evaluated on citation exact-match (section+subsection) over a small, real eval set pending human verification.
AI.result: Results show the base model cannot cite the RTA, SFT-only mis-recalls sections, and retrieval is essential to eliminate hallucinations. The SFT+RAG hybrid scores highest at 0.481 exact-match with zero hallucinated citations, outperforming both larger specialized retrieval models and approaches with improved training sets.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that a hybrid SFT+RAG approach is the most effective for statutory citation tasks, achieving the highest exact-match score (0.481) with zero hallucinations, and that specialized retrieval models or larger datasets are not necessary for strong performance, though the system does not yet meet the aspirational 0.70 target.

title: Judging to Improve: A De-biased VLM-as-3D-Judge Protocol for Single-Image 3D Generation
summary: A companion study established a de-biased, cross-model VLM-as-3D-judge that reliably ranks single-image-to-3D mesh quality where cheap geometry and CLIP proxies fall short. This paper asks: can that judge's preferences specialize a strong open generator, TRELLIS, on one asset class (furniture), cheaply and without human labels? Taking the judge from ranking to optimization is where the work lives. Pushing a VLM judge into the training and evaluation loop exposes failure modes ranking never triggered, so our contribution is an optimization-grade hardening of the judge: a training judge (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) held distinct from an evaluation judge (InternVL3-8B) to break circularity; position-bias correction; and fixes for three failure modes (image overload, geometry-hiding splat renders, and reference-free judging that rewards clean-but-wrong outputs), with calibration evidence (clear-gap win-rate 0.83-1.0; base-vs-base ~0.5). Using this protocol as an independent evaluator, and working only from public models and data with lightweight parameter-efficient adaptation, we find our methods match the strong base rather than exceed it. Independent base samples carry essentially no learnable preference (0.94 order-flip rate), so signal must be engineered by quality-contrastive construction. Across six adaptation methods, two input regimes, and a severity sweep, the most targeted - conditioner repair under severe degradation - reaches parity (0.50) with the base, while no method clears the >=65% win-rate target. The result is mechanistic: clean inputs saturate the judge, flow-DIT fine-tuning washes out through the sampler, and conditioning repair is the locus that moves geometry. Win-rates are directional at n=8 objects. Matching a strong public-data base with cheap adaptation is itself informative: exceeding it needs more than lightweight PEFT on public data, and the judge protocol is reusable.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates using a VLM judge to optimize 3D mesh generation without human labels, developing hardened optimization-grade evaluation protocols, but finds lightweight adaptation on public data only matches rather than exceeds baseline performance.
AI.motivation: Can a VLM judge's preferences be used to specialize a strong open 3D generator (TRELLIS) on furniture without expensive human labels? The goal is to take ranking capabilities into optimization pipelines cheaply.
AI.method: Developed optimization-grade hardening of VLM judge by separating training (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) and evaluation (InternVL3-8B) judges, applying position-bias correction, and fixing three failure modes (image overload, geometry-hiding splat renders, reference-free judging). Tested six adaptation methods across two input regimes and severity sweeps.
AI.result: Lightweight PEFT adaptation on public data only matches baseline performance (0.50 win-rate). No method achieved the target >=65% win-rate. Conditioner repair under severe degradation reached parity with base, while clean inputs saturated the judge and flow-DIT fine-tuning washed out through sampling.
AI.conclusion: Matching a strong public-data base with cheap adaptation is informative but exceeding it requires more than lightweight PEFT on public data. The judge protocol developed is reusable for future optimization efforts, revealing mechanistic insights about where optimization signal can be found.

title: CRAX: Fast Safe Reinforcement Learning Benchmarking
summary: Safety is a core concern for deploying reinforcement learning (RL) agents in real-world domains such as robotics and autonomous driving. While benchmarks have been central to progress in RL, existing safety benchmarks with high-fidelity 3D physics remain computationally slow, limiting large-scale experimentation and rapid prototyping. To address this gap, we propose CRAX (Constrained RL Accelerated with JAX). Built on top of the MuJoCo XLA (MJX) physics engine with realistic 3D dynamics, CRAX leverages vectorized operations and hardware acceleration, yielding up to ~100x speedups over comparable CPU-based safety benchmarks. The benchmark features six environment suites and three agent-specific tasks, each spanning three difficulty levels. Evaluating six popular safe RL methods shows that no single approach dominates across all tasks, and reveals the trade-offs between performance and safety. We find that curriculum learning across difficulty levels and safety transfer can improve performance over direct training in harder settings.

AI.tldr: CRAX is a JAX-accelerated safety benchmark for reinforcement learning that achieves ~100x speedup over CPU-based benchmarks, enabling large-scale experimentation in safe RL.
AI.motivation: Safety is critical for deploying RL agents in real-world domains, but existing safety benchmarks with realistic 3D physics are computationally slow, limiting large-scale experimentation and rapid prototyping.
AI.method: CRAX leverages JAX and the MuJoCo XLA physics engine to create a vectorized, hardware-accelerated benchmark with realistic 3D dynamics. It features six environment suites, three agent-specific tasks, each with three difficulty levels.
AI.result: The benchmark achieves ~100x speedup over comparable CPU-based safety benchmarks. Evaluation of six popular safe RL methods shows no single approach dominates across all tasks, revealing trade-offs between performance and safety. Curriculum learning and safety transfer improve performance over direct training in harder settings.
AI.conclusion: CRAX addresses the computational bottleneck in safe RL research by providing an accelerated benchmark that enables more efficient experimentation and reveals important insights about the limitations of current safe RL methods.

title: Towards Modality-imbalanced Federated Graph Learning: A Data Synthesis-based Approach
summary: MultiModal Federated Graph Learning (MM-FGL) offers a natural collaborative training paradigm, but its practical deployment is challenged by two granularities of modality imbalance. Client-level imbalance occurs when certain clients lack entire modalities, while node-level imbalance occurs when individual nodes exhibit missing visual or textual attributes. While several relevant studies exist, our investigation reveals that they predominantly target graph-agnostic or centralized scenarios, rendering them difficult to adapt directly. To address these challenges, we formalize modality-imbalanced MM-FGL as an implicit graph-aware latent semantic representation synthesis problem. This paradigm recovers missing modal semantics directly within the representation space, thereby maximizing alignment with the original data's semantic distribution and mitigating the high variance induced by missing modalities. To this end, we propose FedMGS (Federated Modality-aware Graph Synthesis), which integrates three core components. The availability-aware graph encoder prevents missing modalities from contaminating local structural propagation. The prototype-guided latent semantic synthesizer establishes cross-client semantic anchors for unavailable modalities. The reliability-calibrated semantic fusion mechanism regulates the impact of recovered latent representations prior to predictive readout. Extensive experiments on four tasks show that FedMGS consistently outperforms competitive baselines with gains up to 17.41% with best efficiency-performance tradeoff.

AI.tldr: FedMGS addresses modality imbalance in multimodal federated graph learning by synthesizing missing modal semantics directly in representation space through availability-aware encoding, prototype-guided synthesis, and reliability-calibrated fusion.
AI.motivation: Two granularities of modality imbalance hinder practical deployment of multimodal federated graph learning: client-level (clients missing entire modalities) and node-level (nodes missing visual/textual attributes). Existing methods focus on graph-agnostic or centralized scenarios and cannot adapt directly.
AI.method: FedMGS formalizes modality-imbalanced MM-FGL as implicit graph-aware latent semantic representation synthesis. It has three components: 1) availability-aware graph encoder prevents missing modalities from contaminating local structure; 2) prototype-guided latent semantic synthesizer establishes cross-client semantic anchors for unavailable modalities; 3) reliability-calibrated semantic fusion regulates impact of recovered representations before prediction.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on four tasks show FedMGS consistently outperforms competitive baselines with gains up to 17.41%, achieving best efficiency-performance tradeoff.
AI.conclusion: FedMGS effectively addresses modality imbalance in multimodal federated graph learning by recovering missing modal semantics in representation space, demonstrating superior performance and practical viability.

title: The Significance of Style Diversity in Annotation-Free Synthetic Data Generation
summary: Generating high-utility synthetic data for intent classification typically requires human-annotated seed data, which is often unavailable in fast-paced industrial settings. In this paper, we propose a framework for synthetic dialogue generation that works entirely without human-annotated data, relying solely on intent definitions. Our proposed dialogue generation framework utilizes two different types of topic and style attributes to improve data diversity. Also, we propose two novel post-hoc stylization models called Univ and Exam to transform synthetic LLM-generated utterances into more varied, human-like linguistic styles. To enhance data quality, we utilize an LLM-as-a-judge filtering process. Experimental results on both industrial and public datasets demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves up to 93.3% of the performance obtained using human-annotated training data. Crucially, the findings reveal that style diversity is more critical than topic diversity for synthetic data utility, as it prevents models from learning spurious stylistic correlations. Furthermore, the study shows that incorporating style attributes during the generation process is more effective than post-hoc style adaptation.

AI.tldr: A framework for generating synthetic dialogue data without human annotations, using only intent definitions and focusing on style diversity for better utility.
AI.motivation: Human-annotated seed data is often unavailable in fast-paced industrial settings, creating a need for synthetic data generation without manual annotation.
AI.method: Uses intent definitions only, with topic and style attributes for data diversity, plus novel post-hoc stylization models (Univ and Exam) and LLM-as-a-judge filtering for quality.
AI.result: Achieves up to 93.3% of performance compared to using human-annotated training data on both industrial and public datasets.
AI.conclusion: Style diversity is more critical than topic diversity for synthetic data utility, and incorporating style attributes during generation is more effective than post-hoc adaptation.

title: Direct Advantage Estimation for Scalable and Sample-efficient Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE) has been shown to improve the sample efficiency of deep reinforcement learning algorithms. However, its reliance on full environment observability limits its applicability in realistic settings, and its requirement to model transition probabilities incurs substantial computational overhead for high-dimensional observations. In the present work, we address both limitations. First, we extend the theoretical framework of DAE to partially observable domains with minimal modifications. Second, we reduce its computational complexity by introducing discrete latent dynamics models that efficiently approximate transition probabilities. We evaluate our approach on the Arcade Learning Environment and find that DAE scales effectively with function approximator capacity while retaining high sample efficiency.

AI.tldr: Extension of Direct Advantage Estimation to partially observable domains with discrete latent dynamics models for improved scalability and sample efficiency.
AI.motivation: DAE improves sample efficiency but has two key limitations: 1) requires full environment observability, limiting real-world applicability, and 2) needs to model transition probabilities, causing high computational overhead for high-dimensional observations.
AI.method: Two main contributions: 1) Extend DAE theoretical framework to partially observable domains with minimal modifications, and 2) Introduce discrete latent dynamics models to approximate transition probabilities efficiently, reducing computational complexity.
AI.result: Evaluated on Arcade Learning Environment, showing DAE scales effectively with function approximator capacity while maintaining high sample efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Successfully addressed both limitations of DAE, making it applicable to partially observable settings and computationally efficient while preserving its sample efficiency benefits.

title: UltraQuant: 4-bit KV Caching for Context-Heavy Agents
summary: Context-heavy agents place unusual pressure on the key-value (KV) cache: long prefixes are reused across many short turns, while concurrency determines whether the serving system can keep GPUs utilized. We study 4-bit KV-cache compression for this setting, using TurboQuant-style rotation and codebook quantization as a quality anchor and vLLM FP8 KV caching as the deployment anchor. We report three contributions. First, we frame 4-bit KV caching around multi-round agent workloads where task quality, cache residency, and serving throughput must be measured jointly. Second, we describe the practical design choices needed to make the 4-bit path robust, including asymmetric K/V treatment, Walsh-Hadamard rotation, QJL removal, and block-scale variants. Third, we present serving optimizations on AMD GPUs, including optimized decode-attention kernels and UltraQuant, an FP4 approximation path that uses FP8 queries, FP4 KV tensors, UE8M0 group scales, and native scaled-MFMA support on CDNA4. On a long-context, multi-turn agentic workload, UltraQuant cuts P50 time-to-first-token by 3.47x in the cache-pressured late rounds (2.3x across all rounds) and raises output throughput by 1.63x over the FP8 KV baseline.

AI.tldr: 4-bit KV-cache compression for context-heavy agent workloads achieves 3.47x speedup in cache-pressured late rounds and 1.63x throughput improvement over FP8 baseline.
AI.motivation: Context-heavy agents pressure KV caches due to long prefixes reused across many short turns, requiring efficient compression to maintain GPU utilization and serving performance.
AI.method: Uses TurboQuant-style rotation and codebook quantization for quality anchor, with practical design choices including asymmetric K/V treatment, Walsh-Hadamard rotation, QJL removal, block-scale variants, and serving optimizations on AMD GPUs including UltraQuant (FP8 queries + FP4 KV tensors + UE8M0 group scales).
AI.result: UltraQuant cuts P50 time-to-first-token by 3.47x in cache-pressured late rounds (2.3x across all rounds) and raises output throughput by 1.63x over FP8 KV baseline on long-context, multi-turn agentic workloads.
AI.conclusion: 4-bit KV-cache compression with proper design choices and GPU optimizations significantly improves serving performance for context-heavy agent workloads while maintaining quality.

title: Pseudo-Feature Padding: A Lightweight Defense Against False Data Injection in Power Grids
summary: Deep Neural Networks DNNs have achieved remarkable accuracy in various tasks including their application in CyberPhysical Systems CPS for detecting False Data Injection Attacks FDIA during critical operations However the unique infrastructure of CPS makes DNNs vulnerable to exploitation by attackers aiming to evade detection Additionally the distinct nature of CPS presents challenges for conventional defense mechanisms against FDIA This paper proposes an innovative defense framework that strengthens DNNs against such attacks by introducing an additional input layer that performs padding in the input samples using pseudofeature values derived from the inputs statistical distribution This padding increases the input dimensionality in a randomized and dataaware manner making adversarial attacks computationally infeasible due to the nontransferable nature of crafted perturbations and the unpredictability of the padded structure Our method is lightweight modelagnostic and requires no modifications to the core architecture making it highly deployable in realworld CPS settings We evaluated our framework on critical power grid applications such as state estimation using the IEEE 14bus 30bus 118bus and 300bus systems Experiments under adversarial settings demonstrate that our padding strategy significantly improves model robustness with negligible impact on performance and effectively mitigates attacks that would otherwise bypass conventional defenses

AI.tldr: Proposes a padding-based defense framework that strengthens DNNs against FDIA in CPS by adding random, data-aware pseudofeatures to increase input dimensionality, making adversarial attacks computationally infeasible.
AI.motivation: DNNs in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) are vulnerable to False Data Injection Attacks (FDIA) that can evade detection due to CPS's unique infrastructure, and conventional defense mechanisms struggle against FDIA in CPS environments.
AI.method: Introduces an additional input layer that performs padding using pseudofeature values derived from input statistical distribution. This randomized, data-aware padding increases input dimensionality while maintaining model architecture.
AI.result: The padding strategy significantly improves model robustness against adversarial attacks with negligible performance impact, effectively mitigating attacks that bypass conventional defenses in power grid state estimation tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed lightweight, model-agnostic framework is highly deployable in real-world CPS settings and provides effective defense against FDIA attacks on DNNs in critical infrastructure applications.

title: On the Redundancy of Timestep Embeddings in Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models rely heavily on explicit timestep embeddings to modulate the denoising process across various noise scales. In this work, we challenge the necessity of these temporal signals by analyzing their impact on U-Net and Diffusion Transformer architectures. Beyond empirical evidence, we provide a theoretical framework demonstrating that, under certain conditions, the global minimizer of the diffusion training objective can be achieved without explicit timestep conditioning. Our findings reveal a surprising robustness when timestep embeddings are completely removed. Extensive ablation studies on the CelebA and CIFAR-10 datasets show that these time-agnostic models can maintain high structural fidelity and even surpass their conditioned counterparts in competitive metrics, including FID, precision, and recall. Our analysis suggests these architectures can implicitly infer noise scales from the corrupted input under specific assumptions, rendering explicit temporal conditioning redundant. This study challenges long-standing temporal conditioning paradigms and paves the way for more efficient and structurally focused generative architectures.

AI.tldr: Diffusion models can achieve state-of-the-art performance without explicit timestep embeddings, as neural architectures can implicitly infer noise scales from corrupted inputs.
AI.motivation: Challenge the necessity of explicit timestep embeddings in diffusion models, which have been considered essential for modulating the denoising process across noise scales.
AI.method: Analyze impact on U-Net and Diffusion Transformer architectures with empirical studies on CelebA and CIFAR-10 datasets, provide theoretical framework showing global minimizer can be achieved without timestep conditioning, conduct extensive ablation studies.
AI.result: Time-agnostic models maintain high structural fidelity and even surpass conditioned counterparts in FID, precision, and recall metrics; models can implicitly infer noise scales from corrupted input.
AI.conclusion: Explicit temporal conditioning may be redundant, challenging long-standing paradigms and paving way for more efficient, structurally focused generative architectures.

title: How Transparent is DiffusionGemma?
comment: 20 main text pages and 6 pages of references and appendices
summary: LLM reasoning transparency is a critical affordance for understanding model decisions, mitigating misuse and misalignment, and debugging surprising model behaviors. However, DiffusionGemma performs a larger fraction of its computation in a continuous latent space; does this make its reasoning less transparent? We study this question by decomposing transparency into two components: variable transparency, whether we understand intermediate snapshots of a model's computational state; and algorithmic transparency, whether we can use these snapshots to reconstruct the process by which the model arrived at its outputs. Naively, DiffusionGemma has poor variable transparency: its opaque serial depth, the amount of serial computation that occurs in between interpretable model states, seems at first 28.6X higher than the corresponding autoregressive Gemma 4 model. However, we show that we can map the information flowing between denoising steps through an interpretable token bottleneck with no decrease in downstream performance. Treating these intermediate states as interpretable reduces the opaque serial depth to just 1.1X that of Gemma 4. Algorithmic transparency is harder for diffusion models than for autoregressive models because all token predictions in the canvas can change at every denoising step, giving the model the power to implement complicated distributed algorithms during the denoising process. To begin bridging this gap, we conduct a suite of interpretability case studies, uncovering initial evidence of novel diffusion-specific phenomena such as non-chronological reasoning, token and sequence smearing, and intermediate-context reasoning. Finally, we test monitorability, a key application of transparency that measures whether model outputs are useful for downstream tasks. We find that DiffusionGemma is similarly monitorable to Gemma 4.

AI.tldr: DiffusionGemma appears less transparent than autoregressive models due to continuous latent space computation, but interpretable bottlenecks can improve variable transparency, though algorithmic transparency remains challenging with evidence of novel diffusion-specific reasoning patterns.
AI.motivation: LLM reasoning transparency is critical for understanding model decisions, mitigating misuse/misalignment, and debugging surprising behaviors. The paper investigates whether DiffusionGemma's larger fraction of computation in continuous latent space makes its reasoning less transparent compared to autoregressive models like Gemma 4.
AI.method: Decomposes transparency into variable transparency (understanding intermediate computational states) and algorithmic transparency (reconstructing decision processes). Develops interpretable token bottlenecks to improve variable transparency. Conducts interpretability case studies to uncover diffusion-specific phenomena and tests monitorability of model outputs.
AI.result: Naive variable transparency is poor with opaque serial depth 28.6X higher than Gemma 4. Interpretable bottlenecks reduce opaque serial depth to 1.1X Gemma 4 with no performance loss. Found evidence of novel diffusion phenomena: non-chronological reasoning, token/sequence smearing, intermediate-context reasoning. DiffusionGemma is similarly monitorable to Gemma 4 for downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: While diffusion models like DiffusionGemma initially appear less transparent, interpretable bottlenecks can substantially improve variable transparency. Algorithmic transparency remains more challenging for diffusion models due to distributed algorithms in denoising, but initial evidence of novel diffusion-specific reasoning patterns suggests opportunities for further transparency research.

title: Neural network surrogates with uncertainty quantification for inverse problems in partial differential equations
summary: Inverse problems for differential equations arise throughout science and engineering, where one seeks to infer unknown model parameters from noisy or incomplete observations. Traditional numerical methods for these problems are often computationally expensive, particularly in Bayesian settings where evaluating the likelihood becomes costly for complex forward models and high-dimensional parameter spaces. To address this challenge, we introduce DeepGaLA, a neural-network surrogate for differential equation solvers that provides uncertainty-aware predictions, reducing overconfident inference when training data are limited. To evaluate the fidelity of the surrogate-induced posterior approximations in practice, we show that a short run of delayed-acceptance Markov chain Monte Carlo can serve as an effective diagnostic. Across a range of numerical experiments, DeepGaLA delivers forward-model approximations with accuracy comparable to established Gaussian-process surrogates, while better maintaining efficiency as parameter dimension grows. Moreover, it can incorporate differential-equation constraints, including in nonlinear settings. Overall, these results indicate that uncertainty-quantified neural surrogates can enable scalable and reliable Bayesian inference for inverse problems in complex systems.

AI.tldr: DeepGaLA is a neural-network surrogate for differential equation solvers that provides uncertainty-aware predictions for Bayesian inverse problems, with accuracy comparable to Gaussian-process surrogates while maintaining better efficiency in high dimensions.
AI.motivation: Traditional numerical methods for Bayesian inverse problems with differential equations are computationally expensive, especially for complex forward models and high-dimensional parameter spaces. There's a need for scalable and reliable approaches that can handle these challenges while providing uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: Introduces DeepGaLA, a neural-network surrogate that approximates differential equation solvers while providing uncertainty-aware predictions. The method includes a diagnostic approach using delayed-acceptance Markov chain Monte Carlo to evaluate surrogate fidelity in practice. It can incorporate differential-equation constraints, including nonlinear settings.
AI.result: DeepGaLA delivers forward-model approximations with accuracy comparable to established Gaussian-process surrogates, while better maintaining efficiency as parameter dimension grows. The surrogate-induced posterior approximations are reliable, and the method reduces overconfident inference when training data are limited.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-quantified neural surrogates like DeepGaLA can enable scalable and reliable Bayesian inference for inverse problems in complex systems, offering an effective alternative to traditional computationally expensive methods.

title: Sparsity, Superposition, and Forgetting: A Mechanistic Study of Representation Retention in Continual Learning
summary: Continual learning (CL) systems often forget previously acquired knowledge, yet the mechanisms driving forgetting remain hard to isolate in practice because real datasets entangle many factors. We present a controlled, toy-world framework that makes these mechanisms observable and testable. Using a synthetic generator-separator pipeline, we define ground-truth latent features, build tasks with tunable sparsity and overlap, and introduce measurable quantities for representation strength and superposition (directional overlap among features). We then study retention dynamics-the temporal change of representation strength by fitting sparse dynamical relations (via SINDy) between retention, superposition, and exposure history. A complementary task-level analysis based on effective rank characterizes how representational capacity is allocated across tasks. Our controlled experiments yield three takeaways. (1) Superposition tends to increase over time with transient dips at task boundaries, suggesting boundary-specific interference rather than steady drift. (2) Higher feature sparsity induces more superposition yet does not inevitably cause forgetting; when representations remain strong, forgetting can be reduced despite overlap. (3) Task-level effective rank grows with sparsity, indicating broader capacity usage under sparse regimes. Together, these results nuance the common intuition that more superposition leads to more forgetting by showing that overlap interacts with representation strength and capacity allocation. Our toy analysis provides falsifiable hypotheses and diagnostic tools for CL.

AI.tldr: A controlled framework isolates forgetting mechanisms in continual learning by using synthetic tasks with tunable sparsity/overlap, revealing that forgetting depends on interaction between superposition, representation strength, and capacity allocation.
AI.motivation: Continual learning systems suffer from forgetting, but real datasets make it hard to isolate the underlying mechanisms. There's a need for a controlled framework to make these mechanisms observable and testable.
AI.method: Developed a synthetic generator-separator pipeline with ground-truth latent features. Created tasks with tunable sparsity and overlap, introduced measurable quantities for representation strength and superposition. Analyzed retention dynamics using SINDy (sparse dynamical relations) and task-level analysis using effective rank.
AI.result: Three key findings: (1) Superposition increases over time with dips at task boundaries, suggesting boundary-specific interference rather than steady drift. (2) Higher feature sparsity induces more superposition but doesn't inevitably cause forgetting if representations remain strong. (3) Task-level effective rank grows with sparsity, indicating broader capacity usage under sparse regimes.
AI.conclusion: The common intuition that more superposition leads to more forgetting is nuanced - overlap interacts with representation strength and capacity allocation. The toy analysis provides falsifiable hypotheses and diagnostic tools for continual learning research.

title: Evolutionary Two-Stage Hyperparameter Optimization Strategies for Physics-Informed Neural Networks
comment: Equal advising: Daria Pugacheva and Fedor Ratnikov. Accepted to the ICLR 2026 Workshop on AI and PDEs
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) solve Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) by embedding physical laws into neural network training. However, their performance suffers from unstable convergence, training plateaus, and strong sensitivity to architectural and optimization hyperparameters due to the highly non-convex and multi-term structure of the physics-informed loss. In this setting, the outer-loop hyperparameter search is a noisy and black-box optimization problem over heterogeneous parameters, where classical local or gradient-based strategies are easily trapped in suboptimal regions. Evolutionary algorithms, with their population-based exploration and ability to handle mixed, non-differentiable search spaces, provide a more robust mechanism for discovering promising configurations. We propose and investigate a two-stage approach based on evolutionary algorithms that combines exploration and exploitation parts of PINNs training to improve solution accuracy and robustness under fixed computational budgets. In the first stage, we perform low-fidelity training runs with truncated epochs to rapidly screen candidate configurations, treating hyperparameter selection as a black-box outer-loop problem. In the second stage, only the most promising candidates are fully trained with standard gradient-based optimizers to refine the solution. Evaluated on three popular problems, namely Advection, Klein-Gordon and Helmholtz equations, our method consistently outperforms standard training and achieves significantly lower mean error within constrained computational resources.

AI.tldr: Evolutionary algorithm-based two-stage approach for optimizing Physics-Informed Neural Network hyperparameters, combining exploration via population-based search with exploitation via full gradient training to improve convergence and accuracy under computational constraints.
AI.motivation: PINNs suffer from unstable convergence, training plateaus, and sensitivity to hyperparameters due to non-convex, multi-term physics-informed loss functions. Traditional hyperparameter optimization methods get trapped in suboptimal regions, requiring more robust approaches for mixed, non-differentiable search spaces.
AI.method: Two-stage evolutionary approach: Stage 1 uses low-fidelity training (truncated epochs) for rapid screening of candidate configurations via population-based evolutionary search. Stage 2 performs full gradient-based training only on the most promising configurations to refine solutions.
AI.result: Evaluated on Advection, Klein-Gordon, and Helmholtz equations, the method consistently outperforms standard training, achieving significantly lower mean error within constrained computational resources.
AI.conclusion: Evolutionary algorithms provide effective exploration for PINNs hyperparameter optimization when combined with gradient-based exploitation, offering improved robustness and accuracy under fixed computational budgets.

title: Data Bias Mitigation under Coverage Constraints & The Price of Fairness
summary: Machine learning models have been shown to exhibit discriminatory outcomes or degraded performance for individuals at the intersection of multiple sensitive attributes, such as race and gender. This stems in part from two interrelated challenges: the lack of principled measures for quantifying bias (potentially intersectional), and insufficient representation of intersectional subgroups in training data. We extend a recent bias mitigation framework to incorporate coverage constraints that enforce sufficient representation across groups, including intersectional subgroups. Since achieving exactly zero bias for all groups may not be data efficient (meaning it may require large amounts of data), our solution trades small approximation errors in bias for greater data efficiency while satisfying coverage constraints. We also formulate bias mitigation as an integer linear program that optimizes over all mitigation strategies, and characterize the price of fairness, the minimum data modification cost, as a function of fairness tolerance. This is essential both for legal compliance, where regulations may mandate specific fairness thresholds, and for data governance, enabling practitioners to make informed trade-offs between bias reduction and data modification (particularly, data purchasing) costs. We evaluate our techniques on publicly available datasets, demonstrating that bias mitigation via our framework preserves predictive accuracy across multiple classifiers, and that coverage constraints, while motivated by statistical considerations, are essential for preserving downstream ML performance.

AI.tldr: Extends bias mitigation framework with coverage constraints to ensure representation of intersectional subgroups, trading small bias errors for data efficiency via integer linear programming.
AI.motivation: ML models exhibit discriminatory outcomes for intersectional groups (e.g., race × gender) due to lack of principled bias measures and insufficient representation of intersectional subgroups in training data.
AI.method: Extends bias mitigation framework with coverage constraints for intersectional subgroup representation; formulates bias mitigation as integer linear program optimizing over all mitigation strategies; trades small bias approximation errors for data efficiency; characterizes price of fairness (minimum data modification cost) as function of fairness tolerance.
AI.result: Evaluation on public datasets shows bias mitigation preserves predictive accuracy across multiple classifiers; coverage constraints are essential for preserving downstream ML performance despite being motivated by statistical considerations.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables compliance with legal fairness thresholds and informed trade-offs between bias reduction and data modification costs (e.g., data purchasing), addressing both legal compliance and data governance needs.

title: Agentic Symbolic Search: Characterizing PDEs Beyond Hand-crafted Expressions, Meshes, and Neural Networks
summary: Mathematicians understand a PDE solution through mathematical structures rather than tables of computed values. Historically, this has been the product of mathematical analysis, carried out by hand for each problem individually. Neither numerical simulation nor neural networks produce those structures directly. We propose Agentic Symbolic Search (ASYS), a prior-guided framework in which an agent translates PDE theory, public problem constraints, and accumulated search experience into testable differentiable symbolic programs. The mathematical forms are refined under evolutionary search, while their continuous parameters are fit by gradient-based optimization. This makes the search an automated form of inductive-bias injection rather than blind symbolic regression. For problems with known analytical forms, ASYS recovers these forms naturally; for other problems, ASYS constructs analytical approximations which can guide mathematicians toward further analysis. In our experiments, across five problems spanning bounded dynamics, finite-time blow-up, and free-boundary focusing, ASYS produces interpretable representations, including a geometric interface formula for Allen-Cahn 2D dynamics and a nine-parameter contraction law for Keller-Segel chemotactic blow-up, in settings where no closed-form description was previously available. ASYS shows the possibility of a new paradigm for characterizing PDE solutions, beyond handcrafted analytical solutions, mesh-based numerical solutions, and neural network approximations.

AI.tldr: ASYS is an automated framework that combines evolutionary search and gradient optimization to discover mathematical structures and analytical approximations for PDE solutions, going beyond traditional numerical methods.
AI.motivation: Mathematicians understand PDE solutions through mathematical structures, but current methods like numerical simulation or neural networks don't produce these structures directly. There's a need for automated discovery of analytical forms that can guide mathematical analysis.
AI.method: Agentic Symbolic Search (ASYS) uses prior-guided framework where an agent translates PDE theory, problem constraints, and search experience into testable differentiable symbolic programs. Mathematical forms are refined via evolutionary search while continuous parameters are optimized with gradient-based methods.
AI.result: ASYS recovers known analytical forms for problems where they exist, and constructs analytical approximations where closed-form solutions are unavailable. The framework produced interpretable representations including a geometric interface formula for Allen-Cahn 2D dynamics and a nine-parameter contraction law for Keller-Segel chemotactic blow-up.
AI.conclusion: ASYS represents a new paradigm for characterizing PDE solutions, bridging the gap between handcrafted analytical solutions, numerical methods, and neural networks, enabling automated discovery of mathematical structures that can guide further mathematical analysis.

title: Fisher-Geometric Sharpness and the Implicit Bias of SGD toward Flat Minima
summary: A widely held intuition in deep learning is that stochastic gradient descent (SGD) implicitly favors flat minima and that flat minima generalize better, but standard Euclidean measures of flatness such as the trace or maximum eigenvalue of the loss Hessian are not invariant under reparametrizations that preserve the network function, which undermines the theoretical foundations of this narrative. In this study we resolve this issue by grounding flatness in the Riemannian geometry of the statistical manifold induced by the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM). We define Riemannian sharpness mathematically and prove that it is invariant under smooth, function-preserving reparametrizations, which directly addresses the critique of Dinh et al. in the paper ``Sharp minima can generalize for deep nets''.We note that this invariance is a property of the true FIM; the diagonal empirical estimator used in practice (and in all experiments below) inherits invariance only approximately, and exact invariance under arbitrary reparametrizations would require structured estimators such as K-FAC. We formalize the gradient noise of mini-batch SGD as having a covariance structure proportional to the FIM, derive the stationary distribution of the resulting stochastic differential equation, and then show that the probability mass is exponentially concentrated at Riemannian-flat minima. A PAC-Bayes generalization bound controlled explicitly by SR formally links this geometric bias to test performance. Our experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 confirm that SR reliably tracks generalization in ways that Euclidean sharpness does not, and that its scaling with $η/B$ matches the theoretical predictions. Together these results provide a rigorous, reparametrization-invariant account of why flat minima generalize.

AI.tldr: SGD's bias toward flat minima can be properly explained using Riemannian geometry via the Fisher Information Matrix, providing a reparametrization-invariant measure of flatness that correlates with generalization.
AI.motivation: Standard Euclidean measures of flatness (like trace or max eigenvalue of Hessian) are not invariant under reparametrizations that preserve network function, undermining the theoretical basis for why flat minima generalize better.
AI.method: Define Riemannian sharpness grounded in the Riemannian geometry of the statistical manifold using Fisher Information Matrix; prove invariance under smooth function-preserving reparametrizations; formalize SGD gradient noise as having covariance proportional to FIM and derive stationary distribution.
AI.result: Riemannian flatness (SR) tracks generalization better than Euclidean sharpness; probability mass concentrates exponentially at Riemannian-flat minima; PAC-Bayes bound links SR to test performance; experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 confirm SR correlates with generalization.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a rigorous, reparametrization-invariant account of why flat minima generalize by grounding flatness in Riemannian geometry via Fisher Information Matrix, resolving previous theoretical issues.

title: Marginal Advantage Accumulation for Memory-Driven Agent Self-Evolution
summary: In batch-style trace distillation, the same memory operation may receive contradictory feedback across different batches. Existing methods lack a cross-batch, operation-level evidence accumulation mechanism, making it impossible to distinguish stably effective operations from accidental hits. This paper formalizes the requirement as two structural conditions, alignability and comparability, and proposes Marginal Advantage Accumulation (MAA). MAA constructs differential signals to make them comparable across batches, accumulates signed evidence per operation via EMA, and ensures cross-batch traceability through semantic identity merging. As a post-processing architecture, MAA achieves the best results in 14 out of 16 settings across 4 benchmarks and 4 target models, consistently outperforming existing batch-level distillation baselines and matching or surpassing online alternatives in most settings, while reducing optimization-phase token consumption by approximately 75%.

AI.tldr: MAA introduces cross-batch operation-level evidence accumulation for trace distillation, achieving superior results with 75% less optimization tokens.
AI.motivation: Current batch-style trace distillation lacks cross-batch operation-level evidence accumulation, preventing distinction between stably effective operations and accidental hits.
AI.method: Formalizes requirements as alignability and comparability; constructs differential signals for cross-batch comparability; accumulates signed evidence per operation via EMA; ensures cross-batch traceability through semantic identity merging.
AI.result: Achieves best results in 14/16 settings across 4 benchmarks and 4 target models; consistently outperforms batch-level distillation baselines; matches or surpasses online alternatives; reduces optimization token consumption by ~75%.
AI.conclusion: MAA provides effective cross-batch operation-level evidence accumulation for trace distillation, significantly improving performance while reducing computational cost.

title: Execution-State Capsules: Graph-Bound Execution-State Checkpoint and Restore for Low-Latency, Small-Batch, On-Device Physical-AI Serving
summary: Mainstream LLM serving systems reuse prefix work mainly through paged or radix key-value (KV) caches. This is highly effective for high-throughput, high-concurrency serving, but it manages only one positional fragment of execution state: the KV cache. We study the opposite regime: low-latency, small-batch, on-device physical-AI serving, where interactive LLM agents, speech systems, and robot policies repeatedly branch, reset, interrupt, and re-enter under tight responsiveness budgets. We introduce execution-state capsules, a graph-bound checkpoint and restore mechanism for the complete restorable state at a committed boundary. FlashRT is a white-box, backend-facing kernel runtime whose evaluated NVIDIA CUDA backend runs captured graph plans over contiguous static buffers with no block-table indirection. Because the live state is a closed set of named buffers, a capsule can snapshot, restore, fork, or roll back the whole execution boundary, including KV, recurrent state, convolution state, MTP state, and metadata. This moves reuse from token-addressed KV fragments to graph-bound execution-state boundaries. On an RTX 5090, capsule restore is byte-exact at the stored-state level and token-identical under greedy decode. A KV-only ablation diverges, showing that recurrent state is load-bearing. GPU-resident snapshot and restore are sub-millisecond, and TTFT speedup over cold prefill grows from 3.9x at 2k tokens to 27x at 16k tokens. On Jetson AGX Thor and DGX Spark, the same correctness and structural properties hold. Capsules are not a replacement for high-throughput KV-cache serving; they define a complementary latency-first serving point for explicit execution-state reuse.

AI.tldr: FlashRT introduces execution-state capsules for low-latency AI serving that enable checkpoint/restore of complete execution state beyond just KV cache, allowing rapid reuse in interactive LLM agents.
AI.motivation: Mainstream LLM serving systems only manage KV cache state, which is insufficient for low-latency, small-batch, on-device AI serving scenarios where interactive agents, speech systems, and robot policies frequently branch, reset, interrupt, and re-enter operations under tight responsiveness requirements.
AI.method: FlashRT uses execution-state capsules - a graph-bound checkpoint and restore mechanism for complete restorable state at committed boundaries. It's a white-box backend-facing kernel runtime with a CUDA backend that runs captured graph plans over contiguous static buffers without block-table indirection, enabling snapshot, restore, fork, or rollback of entire execution boundaries including KV cache, recurrent state, convolution state, and metadata.
AI.result: On RTX 5090, capsule restore is byte-exact and token-identical under greedy decode. KV-only ablation diverges, showing recurrent state is load-bearing. GPU-resident snapshot/restore are sub-millisecond, with TTFT speedup growing from 3.9x at 2k tokens to 27x at 16k tokens. Same correctness holds on Jetson AGX Thor and DGX Spark.
AI.conclusion: Execution-state capsules define a complementary latency-first serving point for explicit execution-state reuse, moving beyond token-addressed KV fragments to graph-bound execution-state boundaries, suitable for interactive on-device AI applications.

title: Multi-Task Bayesian In-Context Learning
summary: Bayesian predictive inference provides a principled framework for uncertainty quantification, data efficiency, and robust generalization. However, exact inference is often intractable, and scalable approximations may remain computationally expensive or require restrictive modeling assumptions that degrade predictive performance. Prior-Data Fitted and in-context models have recently emerged as an amortized alternative by learning to map datasets directly to predictive distributions, but existing approaches are tightly coupled to the support of the training prior and lack explicit mechanisms for adapting to new priors at test time, resulting in limited robustness under distribution shift. We introduce a multi-task in-context learning framework for amortized hierarchical Bayesian predictive inference that explicitly represents prior information as a prefix of in-context datasets. A transformer trained on sequences of prior and target tasks learns to adapt its predictions across families of priors. On a suite of evaluations with increasing difficulty, including out-of-meta-distribution priors and priors with high-dimensional latent structures, our method matches oracle Bayesian predictors while being orders of magnitude faster. We further demonstrate its practical relevance on a real-world spatiotemporal temperature prediction benchmark. Code is available at https://github.com/martianmartina/multi-task-bayesian-icl/.

AI.tldr: Multi-task transformer framework for amortized Bayesian inference that learns to adapt predictions across prior families by representing priors as in-context dataset prefixes.
AI.motivation: Existing amortized Bayesian inference methods (Prior-Data Fitted models) are tightly coupled to training priors and lack adaptation mechanisms for new priors at test time, limiting robustness under distribution shift.
AI.method: Multi-task in-context learning framework where a transformer is trained on sequences of prior and target tasks, with prior information explicitly represented as prefixes of in-context datasets.
AI.result: Matches oracle Bayesian predictors on evaluations including out-of-meta-distribution priors and high-dimensional latent structures, while being orders of magnitude faster; demonstrated practical relevance on real-world spatiotemporal temperature prediction.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides efficient, robust amortized Bayesian inference that adapts to new priors at test time, addressing limitations of previous amortized methods while maintaining computational efficiency.

title: Predictability as a Fine-Grained Measure for Privacy
summary: Differential privacy (DP) ensures rigorous individual-level privacy guarantees against even the most knowledgeable attackers, but its worst-case nature can impose a costly privacy-accuracy tradeoff. We introduce privacy via predictability, a fine-grained framework that explicitly incorporates the attacker's core knowledge, a compromised portion of the dataset generated by a stochastic process, and a specified family of queries. Predictability measures privacy leakage as the incremental gain in an attacker's ability to predict sensitive information about unknown individuals after observing the algorithm's output, beyond what can already be inferred from the compromised data. We show that predictability and DP are generally incomparable: each can be small while the other is large. However, in the worst-case regime where all but one individual is compromised, and all binary queries are considered sensitive, predictability implies mutual-information DP. More generally, predictability provides a finer-grained privacy metric tailored to specific sensitive information and specific attacker models. We introduce a general framework, using the generalized method of moments (GMM), to analyze asymptotic predictability when the compromised data is generated by a stationary, ergodic, mixing process. Using this analysis, we derive a predictability-calibrated output perturbation scheme for ERM. Our approach is complementary to DP and can be used alongside DP to provide fine-grained privacy control.

AI.tldr: A new privacy framework called "privacy via predictability" that measures privacy leakage based on attackers' predictive gain from algorithm outputs beyond what they already know from compromised data.
AI.motivation: Differential privacy provides worst-case guarantees but can be costly in terms of privacy-accuracy tradeoff. There's a need for a more fine-grained privacy metric that incorporates attacker knowledge and specific contexts.
AI.method: Introduces predictability framework that measures privacy leakage as the incremental gain in an attacker's ability to predict sensitive information after observing algorithm output, beyond what can be inferred from compromised data. Uses generalized method of moments (GMM) for asymptotic analysis when compromised data is generated by stochastic processes. Also develops predictability-calibrated output perturbation for empirical risk minimization.
AI.result: Shows predictability and differential privacy are generally incomparable but predictability implies mutual-information DP in worst-case scenarios. Provides finer-grained privacy metric tailored to specific sensitive information and attacker models. Introduces analytical framework for stationary, ergodic, mixing processes and practical perturbation scheme.
AI.conclusion: Predictability provides a complementary framework to differential privacy that enables fine-grained privacy control by incorporating specific attacker knowledge and contexts, offering an alternative to the one-size-fits-all worst-case approach of DP.

title: The Token Is a Group Element: On Lie-Algebra Attention over Matrix Lie Groups
summary: We place the attention token on the group: a token is an element $g_i$ of a matrix Lie group $G$ -- a bare transformation, with no feature payload and no external action $ρ(g)$ carrying it. To our knowledge this is the first attention construction whose tokens are bare matrix Lie group elements: their score is the closed-form algebra norm of the relative pose rather than a learned kernel, and it reaches the affine full-frame groups that every irrep- or surjective-exp-based method must exclude. We call it Lie-Algebra Attention. Once tokens are group elements, the rest follows with none of the usual representation-theoretic machinery. The relative geometry of a pair is canonical, $g_i^{-1} g_j$, so the pairwise invariant $w_{ij} = \log(g_i^{-1} g_j)$ is intrinsic rather than designed; equivariance under the diagonal $G$-action is tautological, and the cocycle condition holds automatically. The attention score is the negative squared algebra norm, $s_{ij} = -\|\log(g_i^{-1} g_j)\|_λ^2/τ$: the canonical proximity kernel under a block-weighted Frobenius inner product, with no irreducible representations, spherical harmonics, Clebsch-Gordan products, or learned kernel. The construction applies to any matrix Lie group on a chosen logarithm chart containing the relative poses, including the non-compact non-abelian affine groups with scale and shear that no vector-token attention method reaches: neither the irrep tradition nor surjective-exp methods. Three sequence-completion experiments, on SE(2), SO(3), and Aff(2), bear this out: the closed-form score matches a learned MLP kernel on the same invariant and outperforms it on SE(2), using 50 to 80x fewer score parameters, while a vector-token baseline breaks invariance by five to twelve orders of magnitude.

AI.tldr: Lie-Algebra Attention: Novel attention mechanism using bare matrix Lie group elements as tokens with closed-form algebra norm scoring, enabling equivariant attention on affine groups without representation theory.
AI.motivation: Existing attention methods for geometric groups rely on representation theory (irreps, spherical harmonics) or surjective exponential maps, which cannot handle non-compact non-abelian affine groups with scale and shear transformations.
AI.method: Tokens are bare matrix Lie group elements g_i. Pairwise invariant is intrinsic: w_ij = log(g_i^{-1} g_j). Attention score is negative squared algebra norm: s_ij = -||log(g_i^{-1} g_j)||²/τ. Uses block-weighted Frobenius inner product without learned kernels or representation theory.
AI.result: On SE(2), SO(3), and Aff(2) sequence completion tasks, closed-form score matches learned MLP kernel performance while using 50-80x fewer parameters. Outperforms MLP kernel on SE(2). Vector-token baseline fails invariance by 5-12 orders of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: Lie-Algebra Attention provides a simpler, more general alternative to representation-theoretic methods, enabling equivariant attention on any matrix Lie group including affine groups, with closed-form scores that are both efficient and high-performing.

title: Optimal Deterministic Multicalibration and Omniprediction
summary:
A model is multicalibrated on a collection of group weights $G$ if it is calibrated -- i.e. unbiased even conditional on its prediction -- not just overall, but also after reweighting contexts by each $g \in G$. It is a useful property for many downstream applications and is a basic desideratum of trustworthy machine learning. Before this work, all predictors known to attain the minimax-optimal $\widetilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})$ sample complexity rate for $\varepsilon$-multicalibration were randomized, while deterministic predictors were known only with substantially worse sample complexity. Whether randomization is necessary for optimal sample complexity in multicalibration was explicitly asked by [CLNR26] and implicitly in several prior works.
  We resolve this open problem by giving a minimax-optimal multicalibration algorithm that outputs a deterministic predictor. We then generalize the algorithm to produce optimal deterministic predictors that satisfy outcome indistinguishability (OI) with respect to finite or finitely covered collections of tests. As an application, this also gives deterministic omnipredictors and panpredictors with optimal sample complexity, resolving open problems posed by [OKK25] and [BHHLZ25].

AI.tldr: Resolves open problem: deterministic multicalibration with optimal sample complexity, generalizes to outcome indistinguishability, enables deterministic omnipredictors.
AI.motivation: Multicalibration is crucial for trustworthy ML, but prior optimal algorithms required randomization; deterministic predictors had worse sample complexity. Open question: is randomization necessary for optimal multicalibration?
AI.method: Develops a novel deterministic algorithm achieving the minimax-optimal $\widetilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})$ sample complexity for multicalibration. Then generalizes the approach to achieve outcome indistinguishability with finite/finitely covered test collections.
AI.result: Yes, randomization is NOT necessary: presents first deterministic multicalibration algorithm with optimal sample complexity. Also obtains deterministic predictors for OI, omnipredictors, and panpredictors with optimal rates.
AI.conclusion: Resolves major open problem, showing deterministic algorithms can match randomized optimality for multicalibration and related notions like OI, omniprediction, and panprediction.
